items.title: 4.0.0-alpha1: build error on loong64

items.body:
## Description

The `timeSpecification` X.509 extension printer in `crypto/x509/v3_timespec.c` has a logic bug in `i2r_OSSL_DAY_TIME()`.

`OSSL_DAY_TIME.second` is defined as optional:

```c
ASN1_SEQUENCE(OSSL_DAY_TIME) = {
    ASN1_EXP_OPT(OSSL_DAY_TIME, hour, ASN1_INTEGER, 0),
    ASN1_EXP_OPT(OSSL_DAY_TIME, minute, ASN1_INTEGER, 1),
    ASN1_EXP_OPT(OSSL_DAY_TIME, second, ASN1_INTEGER, 2),
} ASN1_SEQUENCE_END(OSSL_DAY_TIME)
```

ref) https://github.com/openssl/openssl/blob/openssl-3.6.1/crypto/x509/v3_timespec.c#L54-L58

However, the printer uses this wrong condition:

```c
if (dt->minute && !ASN1_INTEGER_get_int64(&s, dt->second))
    return 0;
```

ref) https://github.com/openssl/openssl/blob/openssl-3.6.1/crypto/x509/v3_timespec.c#L158-L175

This means that when `hour` and `minute` are present but `second` is omitted, the code attempts to parse `dt->second` anyway. `ASN1_INTEGER_get_int64()` handles `NULL` safely and returns failure, so this is not an immediate null dereference, but the extension printer rejects the structure even though `second` is optional in the ASN.1 schema.

## PoC

I created poc certificate based on https://github.com/openssl/openssl/blob/openssl-3.6.1/test/certs/ext-timeSpecification-periodic.pem, removing `second` field in `Daytime`.

```pem
-----BEGIN CERTIFICATE-----
MIICLzCCAhmgAwIBAgIEDCI4TjANBgkqhkiG9w0BAQUFADARMQ8wDQYDVQQDDAZI
aSBtb20wIhgPMjAyMjEyMjExNDQ5NDJaGA8yMDIyMTIyMTE0NDk0MlowETEPMA0G
A1UEAwwGSGkgbW9tMIIBIjANBgkqhkiG9w0BAQEFAAOCAQ8AMIIBCgKCAQEAtnjL
m1ts1hC4fNNt3UnQD9y73bDXgioTyWYSI3ca/KNfuTydjFTEYAmqnuGrBOUfgbmH
3PRQ0AmpqljgWTb3d3K8H4UFvDWQTPSS21IMjm8oqd19nE5GxWirGu0oDRzhWLHe
1RZ7ZrohCPg/1Ocsy47QZuK2laFB0rEmrRWBmEYbDl3/wxf5XfqIqpOynJB02thX
rTCcTM7Rz1FqCFt/ZVZB5hKY2S+CTdE9OIVKlr4WHMfuvUYeOj06GkwLFJHNv2tU
+tovI3mYRxUuY4UupkS3MC+Otey7XKm1P+INjWWoegm6iCAt3VuspVz+6pU2xgl3
nrAVMQHB4fReQPH0pQIDAQABo4GMMIGJMIGGBgNVHSsEfzB9MXUwUaAlMSMwIaAM
MAqgAwIBBaEDAgEroREwD6ADAgEMoQMCASKiAwIBOKEIMQYCAQECAQKiCDEGAgED
AgEEowgxBgIBBQIBBqQKMQgCAgfmAgIH5zAgoQgxBgIBAwIBBKMIMQYCAQcCAQik
CjEIAgIH5wICB+gBAf8CAfswDQYJKoZIhvcNAQEFBQADAQA=
-----END CERTIFICATE-----
```

Then, following command prints out raw dump rather than human-readable text.

```sh
$ LD_LIBRARY_PATH=/home/ubuntu/openssl-3.6.1 ./apps/openssl x509 -in test.pem -text -noout
        (...)
        X509v3 extensions:
            X509v3 Time Specification: 
                Timezone: UTC-05:00
                NOT this time:
                    Periodic:
                        Period:
                            Daytime bands:
                                                0}1u0Q.%1#0!..0
.........+..0.........."....8..1.........1.........1........
1.........0 ..1.........1........
1...............
```

Expected result is following:

```sh
$ LD_LIBRARY_PATH=/home/ubuntu/openssl-3.6.1 ./apps/openssl x509 -in test.pem -text -noout
        (...)
        X509v3 extensions:
            X509v3 Time Specification: 
                Timezone: UTC-05:00
                NOT this time:
                    Periodic:
                        Period:
                            Daytime bands:
                                05:43:00 - 12:34:56
                            Days of the week: SUN, MON
                            Weeks of the month: 3, 4
                            Months: MAY, JUN
                            Years: 2022, 2023
                        Period:
                            Days of the month: 3, 4
                            Months: JUL, AUG
                            Years: 2023, 2024
```

## Suggested fix

Change the guard to test `dt->second` instead of `dt->minute`:

```c
if (dt->second && !ASN1_INTEGER_get_int64(&s, dt->second))
    return 0;
```

items.title: Strange libctx behaviour in PUT threading model

items.body:
We have measured openssl session resumption performance for TLS 1.3 according to observations from [1]
We used openssl-bench [2] as a benchmark tool. We used OpenSSL 3.5.1 series with Red Hat patches not affecting session resumption.

The result shows that, when increasing the concurrency, the following functions begin to grow in time consumption:

1 vs 64 threads
```
$ python compare_amduprof2.py reports/report1.csv reports/report64.csv
                         FUNCTION  CPU_TIME_Base  CPU_TIME_New  Delta_%
            pthread_rwlock_unlock          0.34%         1.87%    1.53%
                      EVP_MD_free          0.19%         1.40%    1.21%
pthread_rwlock_rdlock@@GLIBC_2.34          0.27%         1.46%    1.19%
                    EVP_MD_up_ref          0.09%         1.20%    1.11%
             evp_md_init_internal          0.34%         1.22%    0.88%
 ```

64 vs 128 threads
```
                    FUNCTION  CPU_TIME_Base  CPU_TIME_New  Delta_%
                  EVP_MD_free          1.40%         2.24%    0.84%
                EVP_MD_up_ref          1.20%         1.97%    0.77%
         evp_md_init_internal          1.22%         1.94%    0.72%
```

1 vs 128 threads
```
                         FUNCTION  CPU_TIME_Base  CPU_TIME_New  Delta_%
                      EVP_MD_free          0.19%         2.24%    2.05%
                    EVP_MD_up_ref          0.09%         1.97%    1.88%
             evp_md_init_internal          0.34%         1.94%    1.60%
                 EVP_DigestUpdate          0.12%         1.63%    1.51%
            pthread_rwlock_unlock          0.34%         1.64%    1.30%
pthread_rwlock_rdlock@@GLIBC_2.34          0.27%         1.35%    1.08%

```

We also have compared the performance for 4.0 turning `EVP_MD_free` and `EVP_MD_up_ref` to no-op, connects per second, server/client
```
threads normal_upref_free dummy_upref_free
1       396/365           394/364
33      384/360           402/376
65      322/312           345/333
97      238/233           275/271
127     179/187           126/255
```

The conclusion is: lock contention by itself is responsible for performance degradation to some extent but need to deal with dynamic allocation/refcounting of the EVP_MD objects is a pain point. 

I'm not sure whether OpenSSL should be a competitor with memcached or other in-memory DBs to improve the session lookup, but the corresponding waste of performance are worth dealing with. Probably freezing store (currently a feature branch) would partially resolve the issue.

[1] https://rustls.dev/perf/2024-11-28-threading/
[2] https://github.com/ctz/openssl-bench/

items.title: check_issued() discards specific error code from ossl_x509_likely_issued()

items.body:
## Bug Description

In `crypto/x509/x509_vfy.c`, `verify_chain()` (line 220) converts a `X509_get_pubkey_parameters()` failure to `-1` but does not set `ctx->error` to a meaningful error code:

```c
// Line 220
|| (ok = X509_get_pubkey_parameters(NULL, ctx->chain) ? 1 : -1) <= 0
```

When this fails, `verify_chain()` returns `-1` with `ctx->error` still set to whatever the previous step left it (likely `X509_V_OK` if all prior steps succeeded). The caller `X509_verify_cert()` has a safety-net at line 302-303:

```c
if (ret <= 0 && ctx->error == X509_V_OK)
    ctx->error = X509_V_ERR_UNSPECIFIED;
```

This catches the case and sets `X509_V_ERR_UNSPECIFIED`, but that is a generic catch-all that provides no diagnostic value. The actual failure — missing or incompatible public key parameters in the certificate chain — is not surfaced.

### Inconsistency with other steps

Every other step in `verify_chain()` sets `ctx->error` with a specific error code before returning:

- `build_chain()` — sets `ctx->error` internally on all failure paths
- `check_extensions()` — uses `CB_FAIL_IF` which sets `ctx->error`
- `check_auth_level()` — uses `CB_FAIL_IF`
- `check_id()` — sets `ctx->error` with specific hostname/email/IP mismatch codes
- `X509_chain_check_suiteb()` — returns specific error, applied via `CB_FAIL_IF`
- `internal_verify()` — sets `ctx->error` on all paths

The `X509_get_pubkey_parameters()` call is the only step that relies on the safety-net instead of setting a specific error.

### Impact

When pubkey parameter inheritance fails (e.g., a DSA certificate chain where parameters are absent and cannot be inherited from the issuer), the application receives `X509_V_ERR_UNSPECIFIED` rather than a descriptive error. This makes it difficult to diagnose the specific chain-building failure, particularly with DSA or other parameter-dependent algorithms.

### Suggested Fix

Set `ctx->error` before returning:

```c
if ((ok = X509_get_pubkey_parameters(NULL, ctx->chain)) == 0) {
    ctx->error = X509_V_ERR_UNSPECIFIED;
    ok = -1;
}
if (ok <= 0
    || (ok = ctx->check_revocation(ctx)) <= 0)
    return ok;
```

Ideally a more specific error code like `X509_V_ERR_NO_ISSUER_PUBLIC_KEY` would be used, but `X509_V_ERR_UNSPECIFIED` explicitly set is still an improvement over silently depending on the safety-net, since it documents intent and won't be masked if a future refactor removes the safety-net.

### Affected Versions

Present in 3.0.13 and confirmed unchanged on current master.
items.title: verify_chain() does not set ctx->error when X509_get_pubkey_parameters() fails

items.title: openssl 3.6 breaks the qatprovider

items.body:
## Weakness classification:

* CWE-681: Incorrect Conversion between Numeric Types

## Reviewed Version

* Repository: local clone of openssl/openssl
* Branch reviewed: master
* Commit reviewed: e730ce9
* Version data in tree: 4.0.0-dev

## Description

`EVP_CipderUpdate` (or of it's encrypt/decrypt variants) uses `int` (aka 32-bit signed integer) for the type of the length of the input and output arrays. It then unconditionally cast it to a `size_t` (aka unsigned 32/64-bit integer) in here:

https://github.com/openssl/openssl/blob/05ac67903950f4dfc78a65148cab794410edac32/crypto/evp/evp_enc.c#L650-L654

That cast is unsafe without proper bound checking, as small negative integer will be converted into huge positive integers. For example, -1 will be converted to 18446744073709551615.

Code down the line will read from the input buffer and write to the output buffer thinking that those buffers are bigger than they really are, so the behavior is unpredictably, potentially crashing the application.

Most of other OpenSSL APIs accepting `int`'s as array length return an error straight away when they detect a negative number. `EVP_CipderUpdate` should do the same.

labels.description: Bite size change that could be a good start
labels.name: good first issue

items.title: Security hardening: EVP_CipherUpdate and EVP_CipherFinal should check for negative parameters

items.title: dtlsserver fuzzer crash due use-after free

labels.description: The issue is not considered a bug
labels.name: resolved: not a bug

items.title: FIPS provider uses non-constant-time DH_compute_key by default in dh_plain_derive

items.body:
When I create a fresh untaring of openssl-3.5.5, then run ./Configure enable-fips, then make diff-fips-checksum, there is a failure and a resulting diff where crypto/params_idx.c is replaced by crypto/params_idx.c.in. 

I haven't yet reproduced on master or a different release branch.
items.title: 3.5.5 has broken make diff-fips-checksum out of box

items.body:
The Windows on ARM platform now supports three distinct binary formats that can run simultaneously:
*   **ARM64:** Native ARM64. Only native ARM64 applications can load ARM64 shared libraries.
*   **ARM64EC:** (Emulation Compatible) Binaries that allow mixing x64 and ARM64 code. ARM64EC shared libraries can be loaded by both ARM64EC binaries and emulated x64 binaries.
*   **ARM64X:** A combined universal binary containing both ARM64 and ARM64EC code (similar to macOS Universal binaries).

**The Problem:**
Currently, the OpenSSL build process produces shared libraries with ambiguous suffix (`-arm64`) for this target. For instance, seeing `libcrypto-3-arm64.dll` and `libssl-3-arm64.dll` introduces several issues:
1.  **Incompatibility:** It is impossible to distinguish whether a DLL was built for native `arm64` or `arm64ec`. Loading the wrong one results in application crashes or load failures.
2.  **Developer Confusion:** It is unclear to consumers of the binaries which Windows ARM platform the DLLs are actually targeting.

**Proposed Solutions:**
To resolve this confusion and prevent runtime failures, the OpenSSL build system needs a clear naming convention. I propose one of the following two solutions:

**Solution 1: Use separate build targets with dedicated suffixes**
*   **ARM64 target:** Continue producing shared libraries with the `-arm64` suffix.
*   **ARM64EC target:** Produce shared libraries with the `-x64` suffix. While this might sound counter-intuitive at first, it makes sense practically: `arm64ec` is ABI-compatible with `x64`. Applications running in x64 emulation mode and native `arm64ec` applications will both load the same OpenSSL libraries. Building it as ARM64EC but naming it `-x64` ensures compatibility while providing a massive performance boost to emulated x64 applications.

**Solution 2: Produce universal ARM64X DLLs**
*   Build the DLLs as **ARM64X** (combining both architectures) and use the `-x64` suffix. This makes the single set of DLLs compatible across the binary models.
items.title: Ambiguous Windows ARM DLL naming (arm64 vs arm64ec) causes incompatibility and confusion

items.body:
A security-focused code review of `crypto/x509/x509_vfy.c` (OpenSSL 3.0.13, also checked against master) identified 7 issues. Two have already been reported with PoCs (#30465, #30470). The remainder are lower-severity defensive coding gaps. Posting this as a consolidated reference.

---

## Issue 1: Unchecked X509_self_signed() in build_chain() — HIGH

**Line 3234** — Reported as #30465 with PoC

```c
issuer = (X509_self_signed(curr, 0) > 0 || num > max_depth) ?
    NULL : find_issuer(ctx, sk_untrusted, curr);
```

`X509_self_signed()` returns -1 on error (e.g., unrecognized public key algorithm). The `> 0` comparison treats -1 identically to 0 ("not self-signed"), causing `find_issuer()` to proceed on a certificate that should have triggered an internal error.

Contrast with correct handling at line 3111:
```c
int self_signed = X509_self_signed(curr, 0);
if (self_signed < 0) {
    X509_free(issuer);
    goto int_err;
}
```

## Issue 2: Unchecked X509_get_pubkey_parameters() in verify_chain() — MEDIUM

**Line 220** — Reported as #30470

```c
|| (ok = X509_get_pubkey_parameters(NULL, ctx->chain) ? 1 : -1) <= 0
```

When this fails, `ok` is set to -1 but `ctx->error` is not set. Every other step in `verify_chain()` sets a specific error code. This is the only one that relies on the safety-net at line 302 (`X509_V_ERR_UNSPECIFIED`).

## Issue 3: "Sadly, returns 0 also on internal error" — DESIGN

**Lines 907, 931, 1741**

Three functions carry this comment: `check_revocation()`, `check_cert()`, and `internal_verify()`. They return 0 for both "verification failed" and "internal error" (OOM, crypto failure). Callers cannot distinguish them.

This means an OOM during CRL processing is treated as "CRL check failed" — the verify callback may choose to continue, effectively bypassing CRL checking due to a transient error.

## Issue 4: Potential NULL chain access in verify_cb_cert() — LOW

**Line 160**

```c
ctx->current_cert = (x != NULL) ? x : sk_X509_value(ctx->chain, depth);
```

If `x` is NULL and `depth` is out of bounds, `sk_X509_value()` returns NULL, and `ctx->current_cert` is NULL. Verify callback code may dereference it. No defensive bounds check on `depth`.

## Issue 5: CRL_CHECK_ALL with partial chain — NULL issuer — LOW

**Lines 908-929**

When `CRL_CHECK_ALL` is set, the loop includes the trust anchor. `check_cert()` sets `ctx->current_issuer = NULL` (line 940). If CRL verification for the trust anchor needs the issuer (indirect CRLs), NULL propagates. Same pattern as the OCSP NULL issuer crash on master (#29669), but the CRL code handles NULL more gracefully.

## Issue 6: Unconditional ok=1 after delta CRL check — LOW

**Line 973**

```c
} else {
    ok = 1;  /* unconditional success when no delta CRL */
}
```

When no delta CRL exists, `ok` is forced to 1, masking the return value of the preceding `ctx->check_crl()`. Logically correct today, but fragile if the flow changes.

## Issue 7: Potentially stale num in final error callback — LOW

**Line 3294**

```c
return verify_cb_cert(ctx, NULL, num - 1, ctx->error);
```

The alternate-chain logic (lines 3131-3150) can truncate the chain via `sk_X509_pop()`. The local `num` variable and `sk_X509_num(ctx->chain)` could diverge if the exit path doesn't update `num` consistently, potentially referencing a freed certificate slot.

---

## Systemic observation

The biggest pattern across this file is the conflation of "verification failed" (0) and "internal error" (-1). Functions that should return -1 on OOM/corruption instead return 0, which callers interpret as a normal verification failure. A refactor to consistently return -1 on internal errors would eliminate an entire class of error-masking bugs.
items.title: Code Review of x509_vfy.c — FYI Only

items.body:
There are statements in the man page that `X509_CRL_delete_ext()`/`X509_REVOKED_add_ext()` "otherwise identical to the X509v3 functions", which is not correct, as those use `X509v3_delete_extension()` and not `X509v3_delete_ext()` after [1] (or manually removed the extension before). The man page needs to be updated to reflect that discrepancy/change.

[1] https://github.com/openssl/openssl/pull/30350
items.title: Update doc/man3/X509v3_get_ext_by_NID.pod to document changes in #30350

items.body:
My application uses SSL on nonblocking sockets on FreeBSD.  After the remote side closes its socket, `poll()` returns 1 to indicate that the socket is readable.  Then `read()` returns 0 to indicate EOF.  From the command line, `netstat` reports that the socket is in the `CLOSE_WAIT`  condition, indicating that the other side has closed it.  But at the SSL level, `SSL_read` returns -1, indicating an error.  Then `SSL_get_error` returns `SSL_ERROR_WANT_READ`, indicating that there isn't any application level data available, and the application should poll the socket and try again.  But the exact same thing happens when we try again.  This loop repeats forever.

Versions Affected
==============

| FreeBSD version | OpenSSL version  | OpenSSL source | Result |
| --------------- | ---------------- | -------------- | ------ |
| 15.0            | 3.5.4            | FreeBSD        | Buggy  |
| 15.0            | 3.3.6            | ports          | Buggy  |
| 15.0            | 3.2.5            | ports          | Buggy  |
| 15.0            | 3.2.0            | ports          | Buggy  |
| 15.0            | e2d5742b         | Github         | Buggy  |
| 15.0            | 34a4068c         | Github         | Good   |
| 15.0            | 3.1.8            | ports          | Good   |
| 15.0            | 3.1.4            | ports          | Good   |
| 14.3            | 3.0.16           | FreeBSD        | Good   |

Root Cause
=========

I bisected the bug to revision e2d5742b, and found that it replaced a `<=` with a `<`:

Old code: https://github.com/openssl/openssl/blob/0c974fc754e4b0525819ca9f6c3e124141b690ad/ssl/record/rec_layer_s3.c#L330
New code: https://github.com/openssl/openssl/blob/e2d5742b1460c45bf39094ea08e4e85a8f507ea8/ssl/record/tlsrecord.c#L272

Reverting that change fixes the bug for me.

Steps to Reproduce
================

Unfortunately, I only know how to reproduce this problem using my own proprietary application.

See Also
=======

At least two other users have reported the same problem:
* https://github.com/openssl/openssl/discussions/21924
* https://github.com/openssl/openssl/discussions/25323
items.title: SSL_read returns SSL_ERROR_WANT_READ forever on a closed socket

items.body:
## Summary of the bug

In multiple cipher `dupctx` implementations, the destination context is created via a shallow copy (`OPENSSL_memdup` or `*dctx = *sctx`) without deep-copying the heap-allocated `tlsmac` buffer. This causes both the original and duplicated context to share the same `tlsmac` pointer with `alloced = 1`. When both contexts are freed, `ossl_cipher_generic_reset_ctx` calls `OPENSSL_free(ctx->tlsmac)` twice on the same address, resulting in a double-free.

The `tlsmac` buffer is heap-allocated during TLS CBC decryption: `EVP_DecryptUpdate` → `ossl_cipher_generic_block_update` → `ossl_cipher_tlsunpadblock` → `ssl3_cbc_copy_mac` (`tls_pad.c:245`) calls `OPENSSL_malloc(mac_size)` and sets `alloced = 1`.

Note that `chacha20_poly1305_dupctx` (`cipher_chacha20_poly1305.c:64–80`) already handles this correctly by calling `OPENSSL_memdup` to deep-copy `tlsmac` after the shallow copy, but the same pattern was not applied to the other cipher implementations listed above.

## Affected code

The following `dupctx` implementations are affected:

| File | Function |
|------|----------|
| `providers/implementations/ciphers/cipher_aes.c:35` | `aes_dupctx` (via `IMPLEMENT_CIPHER_HW_COPYCTX` → `*dctx = *sctx`) |
| `providers/implementations/ciphers/cipher_aes_cbc_hmac_sha.c:317` | `aes_cbc_hmac_sha1_dupctx` |
| `providers/implementations/ciphers/cipher_aes_cbc_hmac_sha.c:363` | `aes_cbc_hmac_sha256_dupctx` |
| `providers/implementations/ciphers/cipher_rc4_hmac_md5.c:82` | `rc4_hmac_md5_dupctx` |
| `providers/implementations/ciphers/cipher_aes_cbc_hmac_sha_etm.c:210,247,284` | `aes_cbc_hmac_sha{1,256,512}_etm_dupctx` |

The free site is in `providers/implementations/ciphers/ciphercommon.c:142–149`:

```c
void ossl_cipher_generic_reset_ctx(PROV_CIPHER_CTX *ctx)
{
    if (ctx != NULL && ctx->alloced) {
        OPENSSL_free(ctx->tlsmac);
        ctx->alloced = 0;
        ctx->tlsmac = NULL;
    }
}
```

The shallow copy in `aes_dupctx` (via `IMPLEMENT_CIPHER_HW_COPYCTX` in `ciphercommon.h:345`):

```c
// Expands to:
*dctx = *sctx;   // shallow copy — tlsmac pointer and alloced flag are copied verbatim
```

## Tested version

Tested on commit:

`81cc6cb97ef83ad138eebd47129368b9e963e8cd`

## Reproducer output

Built OpenSSL with ASan enabled, then ran the attached PoC. ASan reports a double-free:

```
=================================================================
==2757459==ERROR: AddressSanitizer: attempting double-free on 0x503000023ef0 in thread T0:
    #0 0x7ef1f7ebe470 in __interceptor_free ../../../../src/libsanitizer/asan/asan_malloc_linux.cpp:52
    #1 0x7ef1f6f91e00 in CRYPTO_free crypto/mem.c:331
    #2 0x7ef1f73dae80 in ossl_cipher_generic_reset_ctx providers/implementations/ciphers/ciphercommon.c:145
    #3 0x7ef1f725ee95 in aes_freectx providers/implementations/ciphers/cipher_aes.c:31
    #4 0x7ef1f6ef30a7 in EVP_CIPHER_CTX_reset crypto/evp/evp_enc.c:38
    #5 0x7ef1f6ef3230 in EVP_CIPHER_CTX_free crypto/evp/evp_enc.c:65
    #6 0x596e7cfc09c3 in main poc.c:130

0x503000023ef0 is located 0 bytes inside of 20-byte region [0x503000023ef0,0x503000023f04)
freed by thread T0 here:
    #0 0x7ef1f7ebe470 in __interceptor_free ../../../../src/libsanitizer/asan/asan_malloc_linux.cpp:52
    #1 0x7ef1f6f91e00 in CRYPTO_free crypto/mem.c:331
    #2 0x7ef1f73dae80 in ossl_cipher_generic_reset_ctx providers/implementations/ciphers/ciphercommon.c:145
    #3 0x7ef1f725ee95 in aes_freectx providers/implementations/ciphers/cipher_aes.c:31
    #4 0x7ef1f6ef30a7 in EVP_CIPHER_CTX_reset crypto/evp/evp_enc.c:38
    #5 0x7ef1f6ef3230 in EVP_CIPHER_CTX_free crypto/evp/evp_enc.c:65
    #6 0x596e7cfc09b4 in main poc.c:129

previously allocated by thread T0 here:
    #0 0x7ef1f7ebf91f in __interceptor_malloc ../../../../src/libsanitizer/asan/asan_malloc_linux.cpp:69
    #1 0x7ef1f6f918bb in CRYPTO_malloc crypto/mem.c:214
    #2 0x7ef1f73ea7e1 in ssl3_cbc_copy_mac ssl/record/methods/tls_pad.c:245
    #3 0x7ef1f73ea4b4 in tls1_cbc_remove_padding_and_mac ssl/record/methods/tls_pad.c:161
    #4 0x7ef1f73df070 in ossl_cipher_tlsunpadblock providers/implementations/ciphers/ciphercommon_block.c:165
    #5 0x7ef1f73dbe5b in ossl_cipher_generic_block_update providers/implementations/ciphers/ciphercommon.c:311
    #6 0x7ef1f6ef7354 in EVP_DecryptUpdate crypto/evp/evp_enc.c:791
    #7 0x596e7cfc0938 in main poc.c:109

SUMMARY: AddressSanitizer: double-free ../../../../src/libsanitizer/asan/asan_malloc_linux.cpp:52 in __interceptor_free
==2757459==ABORTING
```

## Steps to reproduce

1. Build OpenSSL with ASan:
   ```bash
   ./Configure enable-asan -g -O1 -fno-omit-frame-pointer
   make -j$(nproc)
   ```

2. Build the PoC: [poc.c](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/26192667/poc.c)

   ```bash
   gcc -fsanitize=address -g -o poc poc.c \
       -I./include \
       -L. -lssl -lcrypto \
       -Wl,-rpath,.
   ```

3. Run it:
   ```bash
   ./poc
   ```

4. The PoC:
   - Creates an AES-128-CBC decryption context
   - Configures TLS 1.0 record mode with SHA1 MAC size (20 bytes) via `OSSL_CIPHER_PARAM_TLS_VERSION` and `OSSL_CIPHER_PARAM_TLS_MAC_SIZE`
   - Performs `EVP_DecryptUpdate` (in-place, as required by TLS mode), which triggers `ssl3_cbc_copy_mac` → `OPENSSL_malloc(20)` + `alloced = 1`
   - Calls `EVP_CIPHER_CTX_copy`, which performs a shallow copy via `aes_dupctx` → `IMPLEMENT_CIPHER_HW_COPYCTX` → `*dctx = *sctx` — both contexts now share the same `tlsmac` heap pointer with `alloced = 1`
   - Frees both contexts: first free releases `tlsmac`, second free triggers the double-free

## Suggested fix

Deep-copy `tlsmac` after the shallow copy in all affected `dupctx` implementations, following the pattern already used in `chacha20_poly1305_dupctx` (`cipher_chacha20_poly1305.c:64–80`).

For the generic case, the deep-copy should be added in or after `IMPLEMENT_CIPHER_HW_COPYCTX`:

```c
*dctx = *sctx;

/* Deep-copy heap-allocated tlsmac to avoid double-free */
if (sctx->base.tlsmac != NULL && sctx->base.alloced) {
    dctx->base.tlsmac = OPENSSL_memdup(sctx->base.tlsmac, sctx->base.tlsmacsize);
    if (dctx->base.tlsmac == NULL)
        return 0;
}
```

Alternatively, centralize this logic in a common helper (e.g., `ossl_cipher_generic_dupctx_tlsmac`) so that all current and future cipher `dupctx` implementations benefit automatically, rather than requiring each one to remember to deep-copy `tlsmac` individually.
items.title: Cipher dupctx: double-free of heap-allocated `tlsmac` via shallow copy in multiple cipher providers

items.body:
We should IMHO not abuse these macros to indicate success or failure in internal APIs. Their use should be limited to direct calls to `exit(3)` or be used as a return value from `main()`.
In all other contexts we should aim to be consistent about returning a positive value for success, and either `0` or `non-positive` for failure.  It may also make sense to gradually switch functions that return `0 | 1` to return a `bool` for clarity.

_Originally posted by @vdukhovni in https://github.com/openssl/openssl/pull/30429#discussion_r2985407700_

items.body:
This is a code cleanup PR.

Per the [coding style guide Chapter 15: Expressions](https://www.openssl.org/policies/technical/coding-style.html#expressions):

> Do not use implicit checks for numbers (not) being 0 or pointers (not) being NULL.

`BN_copy()` returns a `BIGNUM *` (NULL on error), so the check should be:

```c
if (BN_copy(a, b) == NULL)
```

not:

```c
if (!BN_copy(a, b))
```

There are currently 99 instances of the implicit form in the tree:

```
$ ack -h --no-group "\!BN_copy\(" | wc -l
99
```

![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/478aaedc-be9a-460c-9b54-288daa3c9ed0)

("It's an older meme, sir, but it checks out!")

This came up during review of #30530 (comment by @slontis).

It doesn't touch _too_ many modules, probably a single PR makes sense.

labels.description: Bite size change that could be a good start
labels.name: good first issue

items.title: Replace implicit `!BN_copy()` checks with explicit `== NULL`

#     # OPENSSL_TEST_RAND_SEED=1774452402
#     not ok 34 - test_quic_peer_addr_v6
#     ok 35 - test_quic_peer_addr_v4
../../util/wrap.pl ../../test/quicapitest default ../../test/default-recordpadding.cnf ../../test/certs ../../test/recipes/75-test_quicapi_data => 1
not ok 1 - running quicapitest
[17:26:42] 75-test_quicapi.t ........................ 1/? ----------------------
#   Failed test 'running quicapitest'
#   at test/recipes/75-test_quicapi.t line 43.
[17:26:42] 75-test_quicapi.t ........................ Dubious, test returned 1 (wstat 256, 0x100)
Failed 1/1 subtests
```

The v6 test should be ignored instead.

IPv6 stack is disabled.
items.title: [openssl-4.0.0-beta1]: quicapitest is failing in v6 when -DOPENSSL_USE_IPV6=0 is specifically passed during ./Configure

items.body:
### Summary / Description
openssl pkcs12 -info crashes on a malformed PBMAC1-based PKCS#12 file when the embedded PBKDF2 salt is encoded as ASN.1 NULL. The CLI info path decodes PBKDF2PARAM and unconditionally reads pbkdf2_param->salt->value.octet_string, but does not validate that the salt field is actually an OCTET STRING.

### Affected Product
OpenSSL 4.1.0-dev

### Severity
Low

### Steps to Reproduce
1. Build an ASan version of the tree:
```
cd /tmp
git clone --depth=1 https://github.com/openssl/openssl openssl-asan
cd /tmp/openssl-asan
make distclean
./config enable-asan no-shared
make include/openssl/opensslv.h
make -j4 apps/openssl
```
2. Create a malformed PKCS#12 by modifying the bundled good PBMAC1 test file so the PBKDF2 salt becomes ASN.1 NULL:
```
perl -e '
  use strict; use warnings;
  my $in  = "test/recipes/80-test_pkcs12_data/pbmac1_256_256.good.p12";
  my $out = "/tmp/pbmac1_null_salt.p12";
  open my $fh, "<:raw", $in or die $!;
  local $/; my $d = <$fh>; close $fh;

  substr($d,2,2)=pack("C2",0x0a,0x82);
  substr($d,2577,1)=pack("C",0x74);
  substr($d,2579,1)=pack("C",0x65);
  substr($d,2581,1)=pack("C",0x41);
  substr($d,2594,1)=pack("C",0x34);
  substr($d,2596,1)=pack("C",0x24);
  substr($d,2609,1)=pack("C",0x17);
  substr($d,2610,2)=pack("C2",0x05,0x00);
  substr($d,2612,8)="";

  open my $oh, ">:raw", $out or die $!;
  print {$oh} $d; close $oh;
'
```
3. Trigger the bug:
```
ASAN_OPTIONS=detect_leaks=0 \
OPENSSL_MODULES=/tmp/openssl-asan/providers \
/tmp/openssl-asan/apps/openssl pkcs12 \
  -in /tmp/pbmac1_null_salt.p12 -info -noout -passin pass:any
```
ASan Evidence
```
MAC: PBMAC1 using PBKDF2, Iteration 2048
AddressSanitizer:DEADLYSIGNAL
=================================================================
==67310==ERROR: AddressSanitizer: SEGV on unknown address 0x000000000000 (pc 0x56123a9d6a2a bp 0x7ffdef20e6b0 sp 0x7ffdef20d1b8 T0)
==67310==The signal is caused by a READ memory access.
==67310==Hint: address points to the zero page.
    #0 0x56123a9d6a2a in ASN1_STRING_length crypto/asn1/asn1_lib.c:407
    #1 0x56123a74b0a4 in pkcs12_main apps/pkcs12.c:834
    #2 0x56123a73e97f in do_cmd apps/openssl.c:525
    #3 0x56123a6e3a13 in main apps/openssl.c:368
    #4 0x7f45bcc641c9 in __libc_start_call_main ../sysdeps/nptl/libc_start_call_main.h:58
    #5 0x7f45bcc6428a in __libc_start_main_impl ../csu/libc-start.c:360
    #6 0x56123a6eebb4 in _start (/tmp/openssl-asan/apps/openssl+0x32ebb4) (BuildId: 235f4d2b90b98e43e3794d1d329a2a99a99b58de)

AddressSanitizer can not provide additional info.
SUMMARY: AddressSanitizer: SEGV crypto/asn1/asn1_lib.c:407 in ASN1_STRING_length
==67310==ABORTING
```
### Impact
Any workflow that runs openssl pkcs12 -info on attacker-supplied PKCS#12 files can be crashed with a NULL pointer dereference, causing denial
of service. No valid password is required.

### Remediation
Before printing PBMAC1 details, validate that:

- pbkdf2_param->salt != NULL
- pbkdf2_param->salt->type == V_ASN1_OCTET_STRING
- pbkdf2_param->salt->value.octet_string != NULL

The simplest fix is to reuse the same salt validation logic already present in crypto/pkcs12/p12_mutl.c:142

items.body: It refers to no longer existing `atexit` handler extensively, while the only actual effect from not setting it now is non-addition of `-Wl,-znodelete` linking option on Linux and Hurd.

items.body:
In our nightly CI we run `make test` on a fresh built of the OpenSSL master branch with address sanitizer enabled.
Since 2 days this fails with a memory leak finding by ASAN in the `test_fipsinstall` test.

A git bisect shows that commit "Add IKEV2KDF implementation" https://github.com/openssl/openssl/commit/0dd1c50fc0706866800c4ad6e5cd05771be5d0d8 added this memory leak.

It seems that the FIPS selftests somehow cause the leak. Its one direct leak and many indirect leaks:

items.body:
I've just been debugging an issue in the tpm2 provider where OSSL_STORE_load isn't getting the correct key.  The way the tpm2 provider currently works is that all key files can act as both public and private keys (because of the way the TPM stores the key, the public information is always available without the need to send the files to a TPM).  The code in the decoder

https://git.kernel.org/pub/scm/linux/kernel/git/jejb/openssl_tpm2_engine.git/tree/src/provider/decode_encode.c#n75

Currently removes the private key if it's not selected.  However without an OSSL_STORE_expect(), the load function passes zero as the selection causing the decoder only to emit the public key.

Either zero needs to be documented as "any selection" in the man page or possibly store_result.c:try_key_value() should initialize selection to -1 (all bits set).

labels.description: The issue reports errors in (or missing) documentation
labels.name: issue: documentation

items.title: The provider-decoder man page incorrectly implies bits in selection are always set

items.body:
We are integrating a provider-only algorithms and ran into a compatibility problem in OpenSSL when a digest is available via provider fetch (EVP_MD_fetch()) but is not registered through the legacy EVP/ENGINE method database.

Some OpenSSL code paths still rely on EVP_get_digestbynid() / EVP_get_digestbyname() when they only need digest metadata such as digest size or security bits.

This works for built-in / legacy-registered digests, but fails for provider-only digests, even if:

- the provider is loaded,
- the digest is fetchable with EVP_MD_fetch(),
- the digest name/NID mapping exists.

As a result, some X509/TLS flows fail because digest lookup [returns](https://github.com/openssl/openssl/blob/e858e110887a206314036381107ff8223694bacf/crypto/x509/x509_set.c#L279) NULL, security bits remain unset (or effectively become invalid), and verification/handshake logic rejects the algorithm.

The transition from get_digestbynid has already been noted in other [PRs](https://github.com/openssl/openssl/commit/e6c4b93dfcab8a42541debcb567c533ae346566a). This is also required here.
items.title: Provider-only digests are not usable in some X509/TLS paths because EVP_get_digestbynid()

items.body:
As bisected on the `openssl-4.0` branch, ebb188f0d0 appears to cause behavior change in how SSL_write() errors are reported with SSL_get_error() on a peer-closed connection.

Specifically, consider a server application reading from a network socket that has some data with a pending eof.
In this case, SSL_read() returns some data, the next SSL_read() call returns -1, and SSL_get_error() returns SSL_ERROR_SYSCALL with an empty error queue, which is expected if client closed a connection and SSL_OP_IGNORE_UNEXPECTED_EOF is set.

The problem is when the server wants to write some data in response.
Previously, this expectedly caused SSL_write() to return error, SSL_get_error() to return SSL_ERROR_SYSCALL, errno set to EPIPE with the raised error "tls_retry_write_records failure".

With ebb188f0d0 (and b9da8a5844) applied, after SSL_write() failed, SSL_get_error() now returns SSL_ERROR_SSL with an empty error queue, which contradicts the `SSL_get_error` manual page:

       SSL_ERROR_SSL
           A non-recoverable, fatal error in the SSL library occurred, usually
           a protocol error.  The OpenSSL error queue contains more
           information on the error.

The reason is that when SSL_read() returns -1, it is reported in HANDLE_RLAYER_READ_RETURN as `OSSL_RECORD_RETURN_FATAL`, for which ebb188f0d0 sets `s->statem.state = MSG_FLOW_ERROR;`.
Then, it is restated on the `SSL_write() -> ssl_write_internal() -> ssl_reset_error_state()` codepath as `sc->statem.error_state = ERROR_STATE_SSL`.

While this can be handled in the application (e.g. by treating such write error state as EPIPE), I'd like to clarify if the behavior change is intentional.

labels.description: The issue has been confirmed but won't be fixed
labels.name: resolved: wont fix

items.body:
I have performed some analysis of the number of read locks that are taken while doing a TLS handshake, and what those read locks are.

To do this I used an instrumented version of OpenSSL available in my branch [here](https://github.com/mattcaswell/openssl/tree/read-lock-instrument).

I also used an instrumented version of the handshake performance test [here](https://github.com/mattcaswell/perftools/tree/read-lock-instrument).

I then built both and executed the handshake test using one thread:

````
$ ./handshake /home/matt/dev/openssl-write/test/certs 1
````

The instrumentation tells you how many of each type of read lock was taken during the course of the test. The count starts after the first handshake has completed. In the data below we see that 2531 handshakes occurred, of which I was counting the number of read locks for 2530 of them. These are my results:

````
STARTING!!
rdlockctr: 776798
CRYPTO_EX_READ_LOCK: 242880 (31%)
OSSL_OBJ_READ_LOCK: 30360 (3%)
OSSL_PROPERTY_READ_LOCK: 389620 (50%)
DECODER_CACHE_READ_LOCK: 2530 (0%)
RAND_GET_METHOD_READ_LOCK: 38038 (4%)
DOALL_NAMES_READ_LOCK: 25300 (3%)
X509_STORE_READ_LOCK: 7590 (0%)
BN_MONT_CTX_READ_LOCK: 12650 (1%)
RSA_READ_LOCK: 2530 (0%)
X509_READ_LOCK: 2530 (0%)
OBJ_READ_LOCK: 2530 (0%)
SSL_CONNECTION_READ_LOCK: 5060 (0%)
SSL_SESSION_READ_LOCK: 5060 (0%)
SSL_SESSION_READ_LOCK2: 10120 (1%)
Average time per handshake: 1975.503753us
Handshakes per second: 506.200000
Total handshakes: 2531
````

Refer to my OpenSSL instrumentation branch linked to above to match the lock names to the actual lines of code where the lock is taken. From the above we see 50% are happening in this lock:

https://github.com/openssl/openssl/blob/2172e0c098cd8e2f24a0d5f3cf36b6433bf9ef35/crypto/property/property.c#L235-L238

This is perhaps not too surprising and is the target of the work in the [freeze branch](https://github.com/openssl/openssl/tree/feature/method-store-freeze).

More interesting are some of the other results which suggest to me that there might be other targets suitable for freezing.

In particular this lock represents 31% of all the locks taken during the handshake:

https://github.com/openssl/openssl/blob/2172e0c098cd8e2f24a0d5f3cf36b6433bf9ef35/crypto/ex_data.c#L49-L52

The reasons for this is that we take that lock everytime we construct or free an object that supports ex_data (notably BIOs, but also EVP_PKEYs and others). This happens even if the constructed/freed object is entirely internal and never has ex_data associated with it (which happens a lot).

As well as "freezing" the above lock, I think we could also avoid it altogether for a lot of the "internal only" objects that we create.

Also interesting is this lock which accounts for 4% of all of our read locks:

https://github.com/openssl/openssl/blob/2172e0c098cd8e2f24a0d5f3cf36b6433bf9ef35/crypto/rand/rand_lib.c#L258-L259

This read lock is taken every time we call `RAND_bytes_ex` (and other similar functions), regardless of whether we have a custom RAND_METHOD installed or not (which most of the time we do not).

labels.name: triaged: performance

items.body:
If a certificate chain contains a CA certificate with an "excludes" Name Constraint of bar.example.com and a leaf certificate with a SAN of DNS:*.example.com, OpenSSL does not reject the validation when
supplied a peer name of bar.example.com. Consequently, a peer whose name is otherwise explicitly excluded by a CA's Name Constraints can be validated against the CA.

We should document that excludes name constraint can be bypassed wildcard SANs and that if there are excludes name constraints they should also exclude the wildcard.
items.title: Document that wildcards in DNS SAN can bypass exclusions by name constraints

items.body: ### BIO buffer APIs allow negative length → heap corruption, missing input validation (OpenSSL 3.0.2, 3.6.1) **While testing BIO buffer APIs in OpenSSL 3.6.1, I encountered a crash caused by passing a negative length parameter to public API macros.** The issue appears to originate from missing validation of the long num parameter inside buffer_ctrl() in crypto/bio/bf_buff.c. This report is submitted as a bug / robustness issue, not a confirmed vulnerability. **Environment** - Ubuntu 22.04.5 LTS (x86_64) - Kernel: 6.8.0-1050-aws - GCC: 11.4.0 - OpenSSL (system): 3.0.2 - OpenSSL (tested): 3.6.1 (manual build) **Summary of Issue** Two BIO control paths accept a signed long num parameter but do not properly validate negative values: Negative values bypass guard conditions and later reach memcpy() with an implicitly converted large size_t, resulting in crash (heap corruption / invalid memory access). **Affected Code** Heap OOB WRITE (approx. lines 288–301) ``` case BIO_C_SET_BUFF_READ_DATA: if (num > ctx->ibuf_size) { if (num <= 0) return 0; p1 = OPENSSL_malloc((size_t)num); } ctx->ibuf_off = 0; ctx->ibuf_len = (int)num; memcpy(ctx->ibuf, ptr, (int)num); ``` Heap OOB READ (approx. lines 391–401) ``` case BIO_CTRL_PEEK: { char fake_buf[1]; (void)buffer_read(b, fake_buf, 0); } if (num > ctx->ibuf_len) num = ctx->ibuf_len; memcpy(ptr, &(ctx->ibuf[ctx->ibuf_off]), num); ``` **Public API Usage** ``` #define BIO_set_buffer_read_data(b,buf,num) BIO_ctrl(b,BIO_C_SET_BUFF_READ_DATA,num,buf) #define BIO_buffer_peek(b,s,l) BIO_ctrl(b,BIO_CTRL_PEEK,(l),(s)) ``` The large parameter is signed, and negative values are not explicitly rejected in these paths. **Reproduction ** [poc_bio_heap_read.c](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/26566039/poc_bio_heap_read.c) [poc_bio_heap_write.c](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/26566040/poc_bio_heap_write.c) Build ``` gcc -Wall -g -o poc_bio_heap_write poc_bio_heap_write.c -lssl -lcrypto gcc -Wall -g -o poc_bio_heap_read poc_bio_heap_read.c -lssl -lcrypto ``` Run ``` ./poc_bio_heap_write # Result: SIGABRT (heap corruption) ``` ``` ./poc_bio_heap_read # Result: SIGSEGV ``` **Expected Behavior** The API should reject invalid negative lengths early, for example: Return error (0 or negative) Or explicitly validate num >= 0 before use **Actual Behavior** Negative values: Bypass checks (num > ctx->ibuf_size) Are cast to int and then implicitly to size_t Reach memcpy() → crash

labels.description: Bite size change that could be a good start
labels.name: good first issue

items.title: BIO buffer APIs allow negative length → heap corruption, missing input validation (OpenSSL 3.0.2, 3.6.1)

items.body:
Commit https://github.com/openssl/openssl/commit/e4610b43539b added the following check to `OSSL_PARAM_BLD_push_octet_string`:                                                       
```                                                                                        
  if (bld == NULL || key == NULL || buf == NULL) {                                       
      ERR_raise(ERR_LIB_CRYPTO, ERR_R_PASSED_NULL_PARAMETER);                             
      return 0;                                                                           
  }                                                                                       
```           
                                                                            
This breaks callers that pass buf=NULL, bsize=0 to represent an explicitly empty octet  
string, which was accepted by all prior versions.
                                                                                          
However, AFAICS there are legitimate use cases to pass NULL to this function. For instance, in HKDF, an absent salt causes OpenSSL to default to a hash-length string of zeros, while an explicitly empty salt is a 0-byte key. 
                                                                                        
I came aware of this issue by attempting to update OpenSSL version to 3.5.6, but noticed some tests failing with this stacktrace:
```
failed to add parameter data: OSSL_PARAM_BLD_push_octet_string
openssl error(s):
error:078C0102:common libcrypto routines::passed a null parameter
	:0
goroutine 865 [running]:
net/http.(*conn).serve.func1()
	net/http/server.go:1943 +0xd3
panic({0x3abbee0?, 0xc00132ddc0?})
	runtime/panic.go:783 +0x132
crypto/tls/internal/tls13.ExpandLabel[...](0xc0017e15a8, {0xc002388f00, 0x20, 0x20}, {0x3f9930f, 0x3}, {0x0, 0x0, 0x0}, 0x10)
	crypto/tls/internal/tls13/tls13.go:40 +0x3ff
crypto/tls.(*cipherSuiteTLS13).trafficKey(0x7095140, {0xc002388f00, 0x20, 0x20})
	crypto/tls/key_schedule.go:28 +0x8e
crypto/tls.(*halfConn).setTrafficSecret(0xc00079ac90, 0x7095140, 0xc002388ec0?, {0xc002388f00?, 0x20?, 0x3fb275f?})
	crypto/tls/conn.go:235 +0x67
crypto/tls.(*Conn).setWriteTrafficSecret(...)
	crypto/tls/conn.go:1720
crypto/tls.(*serverHandshakeStateTLS13).sendServerParameters(0xc0017e1800)
	crypto/tls/handshake_server_tls13.go:796 +0x4ea
crypto/tls.(*serverHandshakeStateTLS13).handshake(0xc0017e1800)
	crypto/tls/handshake_server_tls13.go:82 +0x72
crypto/tls.(*Conn).serverHandshake(0xc00079aa88, {0x4696c28, 0xc00074efa0})
	crypto/tls/handshake_server.go:55 +0x19d
crypto/tls.(*Conn).handshakeContext(0xc00079aa88, {0x4696bf0, 0xc000664810})
	crypto/tls/conn.go:1580 +0x372
crypto/tls.(*Conn).HandshakeContext(...)
	crypto/tls/conn.go:1520
net/http.(*conn).serve(0xc001618c60, {0x4696bf0, 0xc0006647e0})
	net/http/server.go:1967 +0x1050
created by net/http.(*Server).Serve in goroutine 619
	net/http/server.go:3493 +0x485
```
  
I'm not sure if this qualifies as a bug, or if the change is fine, but in any case it appears to be an undocumented breaking change.

I opened a PR on golang-fips's side: https://github.com/golang-fips/openssl/pull/417 and they suggested that this should also be looked at upstream, so here goes :)

> @nhorman I understand that it's not the case for high-load applications but doesn't it make sense to add API saying "This context is used exactly in single thread" and eliminate locks in that case at all?

That’s a form of peeking, if you’re checking for a pattern through http/grep/semantic_search (if using a model to touch the API hook).  

_Originally posted by @drQedwards in https://github.com/openssl/openssl/issues/30737#issuecomment-4212910965_

items.title: Vulnerable OpenSSL libraries detected

items.body:
### Summary
PR #24275 correctly made ssl_ctx_system_config report failures when conf_diagnostics=1. However, on the default path (conf_diagnostics=0), errors pushed by handler functions (e.g., SSL_R_NO_CIPHER_MATCH from set_ciphersuites) remain on the per-thread error queue despite ssl_do_config returning success.

This causes SSL_CTX_new to return a valid context with a polluted error queue, which corrupts subsequent SSL_get_error calls — e.g., SSL_ERROR_WANT_READ is misreported as SSL_ERROR_SSL.

Affects: master, 3.5.5, 3.0.18 (all tested).

Investigated together with Brandon Allard (@ballard26).

### Call chain
```
SSL_CTX_new_ex()
  -> ssl_ctx_system_config(ctx)
    -> ssl_do_config(NULL, ctx, NULL, system=1)
      -> SSL_CONF_cmd(cctx, "Ciphersuites", "BOGUS")
        -> cmd_Ciphersuites(cctx, "BOGUS")
          -> SSL_CTX_set_ciphersuites(ctx, "BOGUS")
            -> set_ciphersuites()
              -> ERR_raise(SSL_R_NO_CIPHER_MATCH)  // pushed to queue
      -> return err == 0 || (system && !conf_diagnostics)
         // returns 1 (success) but error remains on queue
```
### Reproducer

Full reproducer with config, Makefile, and proposed fix: https://gist.github.com/pgellert/9a2cf58af476b9f0fbb8cb9441d3eb73

#### Without fix (master):
```bash
$ OPENSSL_CONF=bad_openssl.cnf ./reproducer
OpenSSL version: OpenSSL 4.0.0-dev
SSL_CTX_new succeeded (ctx=0x5648c1339540)
BUG: SSL_CTX_new succeeded but left errors on the queue:
40771098117F0000:error:0A0000B9:SSL routines:set_ciphersuites:no cipher match:ssl/ssl_ciph.c:1273:
```
#### With fix (master + fix.patch):
```bash
$ OPENSSL_CONF=bad_openssl.cnf ./reproducer
OpenSSL version: OpenSSL 4.0.0-dev
SSL_CTX_new succeeded (ctx=0x55750e743540)
OK: error queue is clean
```
### Suggested fix

Use ERR_set_mark / ERR_pop_to_mark in ssl_do_config to discard errors when choosing the lenient path. Patch included in the [gist](https://gist.github.com/pgellert/9a2cf58af476b9f0fbb8cb9441d3eb73). I'm happy to contribute a PR with this patch if the direction makes sense to you.
items.title: ssl_do_config: error queue not cleared on lenient system config path

items.body:
Assembler typically do not respect the `-fvisibility=hidden` directive (or similar), as such, there is no way to globally hide all private symbols that are in OpenSSL assembly code. The side effect of this is that on Unix platforms (like Linux), linking statically OpenSSL into another `.so` or executable will typically re-export these private functions, even if the rest of OpenSSL is compiled with hidden symbols.

OpenSSL already has the `.hidden` directive in its Perl infrastructure (which maps to the right directive) and is used in a few places, but not everywhere. It should probably be more widely used.

I have commits for 3.0, 3.5 and master, but only targeting Intel and ARM assembly code (as that's the architectures I needed). I may have missed some private symbol, but it is quite exhaustive. If the solution is accepted by OpenSSL maintainers, I can open PRs for other branches.

items.body:
> Hello everyone I know this issue is closed but I'm hitting a issue that is related.
> 
> In Node.js I have PR that updates the compiler to Clang: https://github.com/nodejs/node/pull/62656
> 
> AIX header files don't ship declarations for sendmmsg and related functions, despite documentation showing they should be included. This appears to be a bug in AIX header files.
> 
> GCC tolerates implicit function declarations, but Clang 16+ treats them as errors:
> https://www.redhat.com/en/blog/new-warnings-and-errors-clang-16
> 
> For now, we claim to not have these functions. The actual functions are available in libc, so an alternative would be to declare them ourselves for AIX 7.2+.
> 
> I agree with @Enc-EE  comment in https://github.com/openssl/openssl/issues/23751#issuecomment-2426097862
> 
> I looked into this a bit more and want to share some information.
> The sendmmsg and recvmmsg are defined in net/proto_uipc.h
> Doesn't look like this file will be included when including sys/socket.h so I included it manually.
> 
> Shouldn't the fix _really_ be adding `#include <net/proto_uipc.h>` in this conditional?
> 
> https://github.com/makr73/openssl/commit/e40a8103f6f1515d932e213f934531ecb2c82199#diff-a818fa04807217bec0c2885bab4598ec7658fb32eac750f1525e2653ef925cc7R71-R75
> 
> Otherwise these functions won't be found and implict declaration would be used.
> 
> ```c
> 16:55:13 ../deps/openssl/openssl/crypto/bio/bss_dgram.c: In function 'dgram_sendmmsg':
> 16:55:13 ../deps/openssl/openssl/crypto/bio/bss_dgram.c:1416:11: warning: implicit declaration of function 'sendmmsg'; did you mean 'sendmsg'? [-Wimplicit-function-declaration]
> 16:55:14  1416 |     ret = sendmmsg(b->num, mh, num_msg, sysflags);
> 16:55:14       |           ^~~~~~~~
> 16:55:14       |           sendmsg
> 16:55:14 ../deps/openssl/openssl/crypto/bio/bss_dgram.c: In function 'dgram_recvmmsg':
> 16:55:14 ../deps/openssl/openssl/crypto/bio/bss_dgram.c:1609:11: warning: implicit declaration of function 'recvmmsg'; did you mean 'recvmsg'? [-Wimplicit-function-declaration]
> 16:55:14  1609 |     ret = recvmmsg(b->num, mh, num_msg, sysflags, NULL);
> 16:55:14       |           ^~~~~~~~
> 16:55:14       |           recvmsg
> ```
> 
> ref: https://ci.nodejs.org/job/node-test-commit-aix/nodes=aix72-power9/62022/consoleFull
> 
>  

 _Originally posted by @abmusse in [#23751](https://github.com/openssl/openssl/issues/23751#issuecomment-4216913708)_

items.body:
Issue #12210 was recently resolved by adding the `static-vcruntime` Configure option. However, this option does not actually resolve the root problem; it merely masks it.

Currently, the `libcrypto` and `libssl` DLLs still import a set of routines from `vcruntime140.dll`, even though the required routines are also available in the Universal CRT (`ucrt`) library set. As a result, shared libraries compiled with the `/MD` flag (the default behavior) still maintain a strict dependency on `vcruntime140.dll`. If the Visual C++ Redistributable is not installed on the target machine, the libraries fail to load.

Here is a snippet from the `libssl` import section demonstrating the mixed dependencies:
```
...
Imports from VCRUNTIME140.dll
    (hint = 004C) strstr
    (hint = 0044) memchr
    (hint = 004A) strchr
    (hint = 0048) memset
    (hint = 0046) memcpy
    (hint = 0047) memmove
    (hint = 0025) __std_type_info_destroy_list
    (hint = 001C) __current_exception
    (hint = 0035) _except_handler4_common
    (hint = 001D) __current_exception_context

Imports from api-ms-win-crt-heap-l1-1-0.dll
    (hint = 0018) free
    (hint = 0019) malloc

Imports from api-ms-win-crt-environment-l1-1-0.dll
    (hint = 0010) getenv

Imports from api-ms-win-crt-runtime-l1-1-0.dll
    (hint = 0023) _errno
    (hint = 0015) _beginthreadex
    (hint = 003E) _register_onexit_function
    (hint = 006A) terminate
    (hint = 0038) _initterm
    (hint = 0039) _initterm_e
    (hint = 0041) _seh_filter_dll
    (hint = 0022) _endthreadex
    (hint = 0035) _initialize_narrow_environment
    (hint = 0036) _initialize_onexit_table
    (hint = 0019) _configure_narrow_argv
    (hint = 0024) _execute_onexit_table
    (hint = 001F) _crt_atexit
    (hint = 001E) _crt_at_quick_exit
    (hint = 0017) _cexit

Imports from api-ms-win-crt-time-l1-1-0.dll
    (hint = 0030) _time64

Imports from api-ms-win-crt-string-l1-1-0.dll
    (hint = 008E) strncmp
    (hint = 006E) isspace

Imports from api-ms-win-crt-utility-l1-1-0.dll
    (hint = 0019) qsort

Imports from api-ms-win-crt-filesystem-l1-1-0.dll
    (hint = 0020) _stat64i32

Imports from api-ms-win-crt-convert-l1-1-0.dll
    (hint = 0050) atoi
...
```


Currently, using the `/MT` flag (or `static-vcruntime`) is the only workaround to remove the external dependency on `vcruntime140.dll`. However, because this statically links the runtime, it introduces a significant supportability and security issue. If Microsoft updates the `ucrt` libraries at the OS level (for example, to patch a high-severity vulnerability), the statically-linked OpenSSL DLLs will not benefit from the fix and will require a manual rebuild.

Since the Windows `ucrt` is now a core OS component and exports the routines needed by `libcrypto` and `libssl`, shouldn't the build system rely entirely on `ucrt` and drop the `vcruntime140.dll` dependency for standard dynamic builds?

labels.name: issue: question

items.title: Why do Windows builds depend on both VCRuntime and UCRT? (Follow-up to #12210)

items.body:
### Version

3.6.2

### Description

An OpenSSL TLS 1.3 client receiving a `ServerHello` without a `KeyShare` extension returns an `IllegalParameter` Alert instead of a `MissingExtension`.

According to the RFC 8446 section 6.2: _missing_extension: Sent by endpoints that receive a handshake message not containing an extension that is mandatory to send for the offered TLS version or other negotiated parameters._ and `KeyShare` extension is mandatory in the `ServerHello` message in this setup, this means that OpenSSL should return a `MissingExtension` instead of an `IllegalParameter` when receiving a `ServerHello` without a `KeyShare`.

### Impact

RFC violation

### Expected behavior

OpenSSL server should send a "MissingExtension" Alert and abort the connection.

### Reproduction steps

Here is an example of a TLS 1.3 handshake that triggers the described behavior :

+ Wait for a client's ClientHello
+ Send a ServerHello without a `KeyShare` extension
  ```
  TLSv1.3 Record Layer: Handshake Protocol: Server Hello
      Content Type: Handshake (22)
      Version: TLS 1.2 (0x0303)
      Length: 50
      Handshake Protocol: Server Hello
          Handshake Type: Server Hello (2)
          Length: 46
          Version: TLS 1.2 (0x0303)
          Random: 0101010101010101010101010101010101010101010101010101010101010101
          Session ID Length: 0
          Cipher Suite: TLS_AES_128_GCM_SHA256 (0x1301)
          Compression Method: null (0)
          Extensions Length: 6
          Extension: supported_versions (len=2) TLS 1.3
              Type: supported_versions (43)
              Length: 2
              Supported Version: TLS 1.3 (0x0304)
    ```
    in raw hex: `16030300320200002e03030101010101010101010101010101010101010101010101010101010101010101001301000006002b00020304`
+ The client should send an `IllegalParameter` alert


Start the following Python TCP server :

```python
import socket

HOST = "0.0.0.0"
PORT = 3000

payload1 = bytes.fromhex(
    "16030300320200002e03030101010101010101010101010101010101010101010101010101010101010101001301000006002b00020304"
)

with socket.socket(socket.AF_INET, socket.SOCK_STREAM) as server_socket:
    server_socket.setsockopt(socket.SOL_SOCKET, socket.SO_REUSEADDR, 1)
    server_socket.bind((HOST, PORT))
    server_socket.listen(1)
    print(f"[*] Listening on {HOST}:{PORT} ...")

    # Accept client connection
    conn, addr = server_socket.accept()
    with conn:
        print(f"[+] Connection from {addr}")

        data = conn.recv(1024)
        print(f"[>] Received: {data.hex()}")

        # Send decoded payload
        conn.sendall(payload1)
        print(f"[<] Sent: {payload1.hex()}")

        data = conn.recv(1024)
        print(f"[>] Received: {data.hex()}")

        data = conn.recv(1024)
        print(f"[>] Received: {data.hex()}")
```

Then start a TLS 1.3 OpenSSL client :
```sh
openssl s_client -tls1_3 -port 3000 -state -ciphersuites "TLS_AES_128_GCM_SHA256" -curves "P-256:P-384"
```
You should see the OpenSSL client sending an `IllegalParameter` alert.

### Acknowledgements

This bug was found thanks to the [tlspuffin](https://github.com/tlspuffin/tlspuffin) fuzzer designed and developed by the tlspuffin team:
- Max Ammann
- Nataël Baffou - Loria, Inria
- Olivier Demengeon - Loria, Inria
- Tom Gouville - Loria, Inria
- Lucca Hirschi - Loria, Inria
- Steve Kremer - Loria, Inria
- Michael Mera - Loria, Inria

items.title: RFC 8446 violation: OpenSSL returns incorrect Alert when receiving ServerHello with missing extension

items.body:
## Problem

PR #27076 (merged in 3.5.0) added parsing support for PKCS#8 v2 (RFC 5958), but the Ed25519 implementation does not yet use the v2 public key field. This issue tracks the remaining work to complete RFC 5958 support for Ed25519.

When loading an Ed25519 private key from PKCS#8, OpenSSL unconditionally derives the public key from the private key bytes via `ossl_ecx_public_from_private()` in [`ossl_ecx_key_op()`](https://github.com/openssl/openssl/blob/master/crypto/ec/ecx_backend.c#L211). There is no check for a PKCS#8 v2 (RFC 5958) public key field — even when one is present, it is ignored. Ed448 shares the same code path and is equally affected.

The specific code path is [`ossl_ecx_key_from_pkcs8()`](https://github.com/openssl/openssl/blob/master/crypto/ec/ecx_backend.c#L223), which calls [`ossl_ecx_key_op()`](https://github.com/openssl/openssl/blob/master/crypto/ec/ecx_backend.c#L248). At [L209-211](https://github.com/openssl/openssl/blob/master/crypto/ec/ecx_backend.c#L209-L211):

```c
memcpy(privkey, p, KEYLENID(id));       // copy private key bytes
ossl_ecx_public_from_private(key);       // ALWAYS derive — no conditional
```

This contrasts with EC keys, where the inner `EC_PRIVATEKEY` structure (RFC 5915) includes an optional `publicKey` field. At [`ec_asn1.c:989`](https://github.com/openssl/openssl/blob/master/crypto/ec/ec_asn1.c#L989), OpenSSL checks: `if (priv_key->publicKey) { use it } else { derive }`. Ed25519's inner format (RFC 8410) is a bare 32-byte octet string with no such field — the only place to carry an explicit public key is the PKCS#8 v2 `kpub` field from RFC 5958, which is now parsed (#27076) but never used.

PR #27076 (merged in 3.5.0) made OpenSSL parse and tolerate the v2 public key field, but as the commit message states: **"Presently any included public key is unused."** The `PKCS8_PRIV_KEY_INFO` struct now has a [`kpub` field](https://github.com/openssl/openssl/blob/master/include/crypto/x509.h#L299), and the ASN1 template in [`p8_pkey.c:48`](https://github.com/openssl/openssl/blob/master/crypto/asn1/p8_pkey.c#L48) decodes it — but `ossl_ecx_key_from_pkcs8()` never accesses it. It only calls `PKCS8_pkey_get0()` to extract the private key bytes and algorithm.

## Impact

1. **Interoperability** — Other implementations (Rust's `ring` crate, Bouncy Castle) already generate Ed25519 keys in PKCS#8 v2 format with the public key included. OpenSSL can now load these keys (#27076) but silently discards the public key and re-derives it. While this produces the correct result for software keys, it defeats the purpose of including the public key.

2. **Third-party OpenSSL providers** — Providers that store key references rather than raw key material cannot rely on the v2 public key field to supply the correct public key, forcing provider-specific workarounds.

**Note:** Ed448 shares the same [`ossl_ecx_key_op()`](https://github.com/openssl/openssl/blob/master/crypto/ec/ecx_backend.c#L148) code path as Ed25519 — same unconditional derivation, same impact, and the same fix would apply to both.

## Current State of RFC 5958 Support

OpenSSL has made incremental progress toward PKCS#8 v2 support, but the final step — actually using the public key for Ed25519/Ed448 — remains unimplemented:

| What | Status | Details |
|---|---|---|
| Accept (not reject) v2 PKCS#8 | Done | [#27076](https://github.com/openssl/openssl/pull/27076) by @vdukhovni — merged Mar 2025 into master and 3.5. OpenSSL no longer rejects PKCS#8 structures with version=1 and an optional public key field. |
| Parse v2 public key into `PKCS8_PRIV_KEY_INFO.kpub` | Done | [#27076](https://github.com/openssl/openssl/pull/27076) — the `kpub` field is decoded via the ASN1 template in [`p8_pkey.c:48`](https://github.com/openssl/openssl/blob/master/crypto/asn1/p8_pkey.c#L48), but the commit message explicitly states: "Presently any included public key is unused." |
| Validate PKCS#8 version field | Done | [#26464](https://github.com/openssl/openssl/pull/26464) by @baentsch — merged Jan 2025 into master. Rejects version values > 1 and enforces that `kpub` is only present when version=1 (v2) per RFC 5958. See [`p8_pkey.c:34-37`](https://github.com/openssl/openssl/blob/master/crypto/asn1/p8_pkey.c#L34-L37). |
| **Use v2 public key for Ed25519** | **Not done** | [`ossl_ecx_key_from_pkcs8()`](https://github.com/openssl/openssl/blob/master/crypto/ec/ecx_backend.c#L223) receives the `PKCS8_PRIV_KEY_INFO` (which contains `kpub`) but never accesses it — it only extracts the private key bytes via `PKCS8_pkey_get0()` and passes them to [`ossl_ecx_key_op()`](https://github.com/openssl/openssl/blob/master/crypto/ec/ecx_backend.c#L248), which unconditionally derives the public key at [L211](https://github.com/openssl/openssl/blob/master/crypto/ec/ecx_backend.c#L211). |
| **Generate v2 PKCS#8 with public key** | **Not done** | OpenSSL always emits v1 PKCS#8 (version=0, no public key) for Ed25519. There is no option to produce v2 output. Without this, any tool or provider that needs to create v2 PEM files must construct the DER manually. |
| **Getter/setter API for v2 public key** | **Not done** | The [`kpub` field](https://github.com/openssl/openssl/blob/master/include/crypto/x509.h#L299) on `PKCS8_PRIV_KEY_INFO` is internal — no public getter/setter exists. Third-party providers cannot construct or inspect v2 structures through the public API. [#13942](https://github.com/openssl/openssl/pull/13942) by @beldmit included this API but was closed May 2024 without merging. @levitte [expressed interest](https://github.com/openssl/openssl/pull/13942#issuecomment-2139468680) in reviving it. |

The three **Not done** rows are the gaps this issue tracks.

## Related Issues and PRs

- [#10468](https://github.com/openssl/openssl/issues/10468) — "Add support for PKCS#8 v2 (RFC 5958 - Asymmetric Key Packages)" — original feature request by @levitte (closed during backlog reduction)
- [#9134](https://github.com/openssl/openssl/issues/9134) — "openssl doesn't accept ring generated ed25519 key pairs" — original interop bug that motivated #10468
- [#14015](https://github.com/openssl/openssl/issues/14015) — "Ed25519 private keys lack public key attribute"
- [#13942](https://github.com/openssl/openssl/pull/13942) — "Very basic support of RFC 5958" by @beldmit — included getter/setter API and documentation (closed May 2024, never merged;
  @levitte [expressed interest](https://github.com/openssl/openssl/pull/13942#issuecomment-2139468680) in reviving)
- [#27076](https://github.com/openssl/openssl/pull/27076) — "Tolerate PKCS#8 V2 with optional public keys" by @vdukhovni (merged 3.5.0 — parses but does not use the v2 public key)
- [#26464](https://github.com/openssl/openssl/pull/26464) — "Add version field check to PKCS8 decoder" by @baentsch (merged, master)

## Proposed Changes

The following items would complete RFC 5958 support for Ed25519, building on the parsing foundation from [#27076](https://github.com/openssl/openssl/pull/27076). Each can be implemented independently.

### 1. Use `kpub` when loading Ed25519 keys

In [`ossl_ecx_key_from_pkcs8()`](https://github.com/openssl/openssl/blob/master/crypto/ec/ecx_backend.c#L223), after the call to [`ossl_ecx_key_op()`](https://github.com/openssl/openssl/blob/master/crypto/ec/ecx_backend.c#L248), check whether the `PKCS8_PRIV_KEY_INFO` structure contains a v2 public key ([`p8inf->kpub`](https://github.com/openssl/openssl/blob/master/include/crypto/x509.h#L299)). If present, use it to replace the derived public key. Optionally validate consistency when the private key is real key material (not an opaque reference).

Since `ossl_ecx_key_from_pkcs8()` is internal OpenSSL code (in `crypto/ec/`), it can access `p8inf->kpub` directly via the internal header `crypto/x509.h` — no new public API is required for this item alone. This is the minimum viable change and could be merged independently.

### 2. Public getter/setter API for `kpub`

The [`kpub` field](https://github.com/openssl/openssl/blob/master/include/crypto/x509.h#L299) on `PKCS8_PRIV_KEY_INFO` is internal — no public getter/setter exists. Without this, third-party providers and applications cannot construct or inspect v2 structures through the public API and must resort to manual DER construction. This was part of [#13942](https://github.com/openssl/openssl/pull/13942)'s scope.

### 3. Encoder support for emitting v2 PKCS#8

`EVP_PKEY2PKCS8` always emits v1 (version=0, no public key). Supporting v2 output would allow tools and providers to produce standard v2 PEM files without custom encoding logic.

Together, these items would complete the RFC 5958 support that [#10468](https://github.com/openssl/openssl/issues/10468) originally requested and that [#27076](https://github.com/openssl/openssl/pull/27076) began.
items.title: Ed25519: Complete PKCS#8 v2 (RFC 5958) support — use, expose, and generate the public key

items.body:
<!--
Thank you for your feature request. If this is your first one,
please take the time to read the following lines before posting it.

NOTE:

    If you're asking about how to use OpenSSL, this isn't the right
    forum.  Please see our User Support resources:
    https://github.com/openssl/openssl/blob/master/SUPPORT.md

Please remember to put ``` lines before and after any commands plus
output and code, like this:

    ```
    $ echo output output output
    output output output
    ```

    ```
    #include <stdio.h>

    int main() {
        int foo = 1;
        printf("%d\n", foo);
    }
    ```
-->

## Request

This issue requests the optional feature to perform X25519 operations, specifically scalar-point multiplications, without "clamping" the private key. Currently, in all exposed operations, the private key is implicitly clamped, even if the bits in the key differ:

https://github.com/openssl/openssl/blob/9ca063ad8455d581aead90727a6ac73069856b51/crypto/ec/curve25519.c#L219-L221

https://github.com/openssl/openssl/blob/9ca063ad8455d581aead90727a6ac73069856b51/crypto/ec/curve25519.c#L743-L745

https://github.com/openssl/openssl/blob/9ca063ad8455d581aead90727a6ac73069856b51/crypto/ec/curve25519.c#L4534-L4536

https://github.com/openssl/openssl/blob/9ca063ad8455d581aead90727a6ac73069856b51/crypto/ec/curve25519.c#L5861-L5863

## Proposed path

The X25519 functions could take a `unsigned char disclamp` parameter, defaulting to `0`. If the 0th-2nd bits (0-indexed) are set, then the 3 LSBs of the private key *aren't* cleared inside the operation. If the 6th bit of `disclamp` is set, then the 254th bit of the private *isn't* set. So a fully unclamped scalar-point multiplication would use `disclamp=0x47`. A scalar-point multiplication with a torsion clear, but without the 254th bit set would use `disclamp=0x07`. I don't think it makes sense in any real application to allow the 255th bit to be set. So the clamp function would look something like this:

```
void clamp_curve25519_privkey(unsigned char k[32], const unsigned char disclamp)
{
    k[31] &= 0x7f;
    k[31] |= (0x40 & ~disclamp);
    k[0] &= (0xf8 | disclamp);
}
```

Since this affects the derivation of the public key, the `disclamp` parameter should probably be a configuration value attached to the key pair (e.g. `EVP_PKEY`), and not an operation context (e.g. `EVP_PKEY_CTX`). 

## Example use cases

Most Ed25519 implementations generate and use private keys without the key clamping specified in the RPC for X25519. Giving the option to use X25519 without key clamping allows one to use an already-generated Ed25519 key pair to do X25519 ECDH key exchanges. It also allows for "inverse" ECDH key exchanges, where knowing $a, Q$ s.t. $Q = a P$, one can calculate $P = a^{-1} Q$. It is difficult to find an inverse of a private key when the 254th bit of the key is clamped.
items.title: X25519: optionally use unclamped private keys

items.body:
As suggested by Tomas Mraz after a private security report, I am reporting a cryptographic logic error in the new **IKEV2KDF** provider. The current implementation of **IKEV2_REKEY** mode in the master branch (4.1.0-dev) does not comply with the HMAC input ordering specified in RFC 7296.

### **Root Cause Analysis**
In _providers/implementations/kdfs/ikev2kdf.c_, the **IKEV2_REKEY()** function feeds HMAC inputs in the order: **(g^ir | Ni | Nr)**.

However, **RFC 7296 §2.18** explicitly specifies the SKEYSEED derivation for rekeying as:
**SKEYSEED = prf(SK_d, Ni | Nr | g^ir)**

The implementation appears to have incorrectly reused the template from **IKEV2_GEN** (Initial Exchange), where **g^ir** is indeed the sole data input, without reordering the parameters for the Rekey scenario.

### Impact

**Interoperability Failure**: Any IKEv2 session using OpenSSL 4.1.0-dev will fail to interoperate with RFC-compliant peers during the first REKEY event, leading to a hard connection drop.

**Test Suite Poisoning**: The existing test vector in _test/recipes/30-test_evp_data/evpkdf_ikev2.txt_ was generated from the buggy implementation, allowing CI to pass silently.

### Environment & Build

**OpenSSL Version**: 4.1.0-dev (master branch)

**Build Source**: This issue was identified and verified using a self-compiled build from the public OpenSSL Git repository.

### PoC
I have verified the discrepancy using a Python-based reference and a C harness using the EVP_KDF API.

**Python Reference Check:**
```
# Result from my analysis:
# code order : 90722C42BD849E9B72D240FC254174270FA8295DE6F0338DAFC303A9EC104F12
# rfc  order : CD1170E00FCCAE2734E804EED069586F305243EE7611F6C380785966C96FB0E0
```
### Proposed Fix
Reorder the EVP_MAC_update calls in IKEV2_REKEY() to match the RFC specification:
```
/* RFC 7296 §2.18 compliant ordering */
EVP_MAC_update(ctx, ni, ni_len);
EVP_MAC_update(ctx, nr, nr_len);
EVP_MAC_update(ctx, shared_secret, shared_secret_len);
```
### Attachments
For a more in-depth analysis, I am attaching the following documents and reproduction files to this issue:
**vulnerability report** (PDF and Markdown) identifying the root cause (CWE-1391) and suggested fix.
**A Python-based PoC** (ikev2_rekey_rfc7296_poc.py) demonstrating the algorithmic discrepancy against the RFC.
**A C-based harness (harness_ikev2.c)** that reproduces the issue using the OpenSSL EVP_KDF API.

[harness_ikev2.c](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/26743809/harness_ikev2.c)
[ikev2_rekey_rfc7296_poc.py](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/26743811/ikev2_rekey_rfc7296_poc.py)
[IKEV2KDF_REKEY_RFC7296_NONCOMPLIANCE.md](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/26743808/IKEV2KDF_REKEY_RFC7296_NONCOMPLIANCE.md)
[IKEV2KDF_REKEY_RFC7296_NONCOMPLIANCE.pdf](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/26743810/IKEV2KDF_REKEY_RFC7296_NONCOMPLIANCE.pdf)

Regards,
Seung Min Shin

items.body:
`https://www.openssl.org/source/openssl-X.Y.Z.tar.gz` currently 301-redirects to a non-existent GitHub tag for any 4.x version, returning a 404 HTML page. 3.x redirects correctly.

## Reproduction

```console
$ curl -sIL https://www.openssl.org/source/openssl-4.0.0.tar.gz | grep -iE 'HTTP|^location'
HTTP/2 301
location: https://github.com/openssl/openssl/releases/download/OpenSSL_4_0_0/openssl-4.0.0.tar.gz
HTTP/2 404

$ curl -sIL https://www.openssl.org/source/openssl-3.6.2.tar.gz | grep -iE 'HTTP|^location'
HTTP/2 301
location: https://github.com/openssl/openssl/releases/download/openssl-3.6.2/openssl-3.6.2.tar.gz
HTTP/2 302
```

The redirect emits the pre-3.0 `OpenSSL_X_Y_Z` tag form for 4.x, but the actual release tag is [`openssl-4.0.0`][tag]. (The same breakage applies to the corresponding signature and checksum artifacts served under the same path.)

Downstream consumers (distro packagers, Dockerfiles, CI pipelines, Renovate/Dependabot bumps) that derive download URLs from the `www.openssl.org/source/openssl-X.Y.Z.tar.gz` pattern silently save a 404 HTML page as `*.tar.gz`.
## In-repo exposure

[`README-FIPS.md`][r1] uses the `www.openssl.org/source/openssl-X.Y.Z.tar.gz` pattern in `wget` examples, currently pinned to 3.1.2 and 3.6.0. The examples work today, but readers extrapolating the URL for a 4.x build will hit the 404.

[`.github/workflows/provider-compatibility.yml`][ci] uses the same URL pattern in its FIPS-validated release matrix (3.0.x/3.1.x today); this will fail whenever 4.x version is added.

## Suggested fix

Either (or both) of:

1. Fix the `www.openssl.org/source/` redirect so it emits `openssl-X.Y.Z` for 4.x to match the current tag scheme. This restores the documented pattern for everyone downstream that already points at the legacy host.
2. Update the in-repo references to point at GitHub Releases directly, matching what [openssl-library.org/source][new] does:
   ```
   https://github.com/openssl/openssl/releases/download/openssl-X.Y.Z/openssl-X.Y.Z.tar.gz
   ```
   Removes the redirect dependency entirely.

Happy to send a PR for (2).

[tag]: https://github.com/openssl/openssl/releases/tag/openssl-4.0.0
[r1]: https://github.com/openssl/openssl/blob/552fe15da5c57975d1238a21e04c779f67963819/README-FIPS.md
[ci]: https://github.com/openssl/openssl/blob/552fe15da5c57975d1238a21e04c779f67963819/.github/workflows/provider-compatibility.yml
[new]: https://openssl-library.org/source/
items.title: www.openssl.org/source/ redirect returns 404 for 4.x release tarballs
