items.body:
SSL_client_hello_get0_session_id(), get0_random(), get0_ciphers(), and
get0_compression_methods() each provide zero-copy access to the
corresponding ClientHello field. No equivalent exists for extensions.

The existing extension APIs have documented limitations:

- SSL_client_hello_get0_ext() requires knowing the type ID upfront
- SSL_client_hello_get1_extensions_present() only returns extensions
  recognized by ext_defs[], omitting GREASE values and types not
  built into the library

The documentation for SSL_client_hello_get1_extensions_present()
explicitly notes this limitation:

> Note that SSL_client_hello_get1_extensions_present() returns only
> recognised extensions; therefore, unrecognised (including GREASE)
> extensions will not appear in the output.

This creates an inconsistency: SSL_client_hello_get0_ciphers() returns
raw cipher bytes including GREASE values, but there is no equivalent
for extensions.

Related issues: #18286 (get1_extensions_present missing extensions),
#27580 (GREASE inconsistency between ciphers and extensions).

### Proposal

Add `SSL_client_hello_get0_extensions()` following the same pattern
as `get0_ciphers()` -- return a pointer into the PACKET buffer and the
byte count. The pointer is valid for the duration of the client_hello_cb.

```c
size_t SSL_client_hello_get0_extensions(SSL *s, const unsigned char **out);
```

This gives callers complete visibility into the extensions present on
the wire, including GREASE and types unknown to the current OpenSSL
version, useful for protocol analysis, logging, and server-side policy
decisions. The returned data uses the standard TLS extension wire
format (type/length/data entries), without the 2-byte total length
prefix.

I have a working implementation with tests and documentation ready to
submit as a PR if this approach is acceptable.
items.title: Add SSL_client_hello_get0_extensions() raw accessor

items.body:
# Summary
OpenSSL does not strictly enforce RFC 8446 when a TLS 1.3 HelloRetryRequest carries an empty Cookie. Under the RFC, cookie<1..2^16-1> forbids zero-length values, and receiving such an out-of-range field should cause the peer to abort the connection immediately with a decode_error. However, the observed OpenSSL client accepts the malformed HRR, continues with a second ClientHello, and only fails later with bad_record_mac, indicating that the invalid empty Cookie is not rejected at parse time as required.

# Cookie Length Requirement vs OpenSSL Runtime Behavior

## Standard Description (RFC 8446)

### 1) Vector syntax and non-empty lower bound
```text
Variable-length vectors are defined by specifying a subrange of legal
lengths, inclusively, using the notation <floor..ceiling>.
...
In the following example, "mandatory" is a vector that must contain
between 300 and 400 bytes of type opaque.  It can never be empty.
```

### 2) Cookie grammar
```text
struct {
    opaque cookie<1..2^16-1>;
} Cookie;
```

### 3) Error-handling requirement
```text
Peers which receive a message which cannot be parsed according to the syntax
(e.g., ... an out-of-range length) MUST terminate the connection with a
"decode_error" alert.

Peers which receive a message which is syntactically correct but semantically
invalid ... MUST terminate the connection with an "illegal_parameter" alert.
```

### 4) Meaning of terminate/abort
```text
... without sending or receiving any additional data.
```

## OpenSSL Code Description

### Client parsing of cookie from HRR
Source: `openssl-master/ssl/statem/extensions_clnt.c`

```c
if (!PACKET_as_length_prefixed_2(pkt, &cookie)
    || !PACKET_memdup(&cookie, &s->ext.tls13_cookie,
        &s->ext.tls13_cookie_len)) {
    SSLfatal(s, SSL_AD_DECODE_ERROR, SSL_R_LENGTH_MISMATCH);
    return 0;
}
```

Observation: this path checks prefix/decode success and memory copy, but does not show an explicit `cookie_len > 0` rejection branch.

### Client construction of cookie in the second ClientHello
Source: `openssl-master/ssl/statem/extensions_clnt.c`

```c
/* Should only be set if we've had an HRR */
if (s->ext.tls13_cookie_len == 0)
    return EXT_RETURN_NOT_SENT;
```

Observation: if stored cookie length is `0`, the client-side construction path simply does not send the cookie extension.

## Runtime Observation

After modifying the HRR cookie to empty (`length = 0`), we observed:

1. Empty cookie bytes were injected into HRR:
```text
... 00 2c 00 02 00 00
```

2. The client still sent the second `ClientHello` (instead of immediate abort with `decode_error` / `illegal_parameter`).

3. Final failure was:
```text
fatal bad_record_mac
decryption failed or bad record mac
```

## Conclusion
The code does not strictly comply with the standards, and when the cookie is empty, it directly rejects the handshake

labels.description: The issue was opened to report a bug
labels.name: issue: bug report

items.title: OpenSSL’s Handling of HelloRetryRequest with an Empty Cookie Is Non-Compliant with RFC 8446: The Handshake Is Not Terminated Immediately

Please remember to tell us in what OpenSSL version you found the issue.

For build issues:

    If this is a build issue, please include the configuration output
    as well as a log of all errors.  Don't forget to include the exact
    commands you typed.

    With OpenSSL before 1.1.1, the configuration output comes from the
    configuration command.  With OpenSSL 1.1.1 and on, it's the output
    of `perl configdata.pm --dump`

For other issues:

    If it isn't a build issue, example code or commands to reproduce
    the issue is highly appreciated.
    Also, please remember to tell us if you worked with your own
    OpenSSL build or if it is system provided.

Please remember to put ``` lines before and after any commands plus
output and code, like this:

    $ echo output output output

I adapted a custom protocol stack to establish a TLS connection, and the program got stuck in an infinite loop. I discovered that the custom protocol stack's `recv` implementation set `errno` to `EAGAIN` when it returned 0. I modified the protocol stack code, and the issue was resolved.  

However, I also noticed that the code in OpenSSL here is not reasonable either
https://github.com/openssl/openssl/blob/6115286faeb8fb023d79660e973a3252b142f6c1/crypto/bio/bss_sock.c#L118-L125
 It should first check the value of `ret` and then check `errno`
```c
 if (ret <= 0) { 
      if (ret == 0) 
         b->flags |= BIO_FLAGS_IN_EOF; 
      else if (BIO_sock_should_retry(ret)) 
         BIO_set_retry_read(b); 
 } 
```
According to the man of `recv`, `errno` should only be checked when the return value is <0.

refs:
https://man7.org/linux/man-pages/man2/recv.2.html
https://alioth-lists.debian.net/pipermail/pkg-openssl-devel/2012-July/003374.html

I am getting a crash with our own build of OpenSSL 3.5.3 on Windows built with MSVC 2022. It seems to be related to the introduction of ML-DSA signature algorithms. Internal certificate array (pkeys[]) contains multiple slots, but only configured ones are valid. 

New ML-DSA indices(s->cert->pkeys[idx].privatekey) point to uninitialized slots containing garbage pointers. (Idx = 11 for ML-DSA)

Code iterates through all signature algorithm slots and dereferences private keys without validation, causing crash when accessing uninitialized entries. 

The issue seems to be in t1_lib.c in function find_sig_alg. From original code below:
```
    for (i = 0; i < s->shared_sigalgslen; i++) {
        /* Skip SHA1, SHA224, DSA and RSA if not PSS */
        lu = s->shared_sigalgs[i];
        if (lu->hash == NID_sha1
            || lu->hash == NID_sha224
            || lu->sig == EVP_PKEY_DSA
            || lu->sig == EVP_PKEY_RSA
            || !tls_sigalg_compat(s, lu))
            continue;
```
I modified it to the following to skip ML-DSA algorithms and it is not longer crashing and work as expected:

```
    for (i = 0; i < s->shared_sigalgslen; i++) {
        /* Skip SHA1, SHA224, DSA and RSA if not PSS */
        lu = s->shared_sigalgs[i];
        if (lu->hash == NID_sha1
            || lu->hash == NID_sha224
            || lu->sig == EVP_PKEY_DSA
            || lu->sig == EVP_PKEY_RSA
            || lu->sig == EVP_PKEY_ML_DSA_65 
            || lu->sig == EVP_PKEY_ML_DSA_44 
            || lu->sig == EVP_PKEY_ML_DSA_87
            || !tls_sigalg_compat(s, lu))
            continue;
```

That being said, I am not sure this is the right fix or if it is something I am doing wrong that leads to this crash. My guess is that the root of the problem is the uninitialized s->cert->pkeys[idx].privatekey.  I don't have that crash in version 3.4.

labels.description: The issue was opened to report a bug

items.title: Crash in openssl 3.5 during TLS handshake

items.body:
## Conclusion

In the "certificate signature verification failure" scenario, the TLS 1.3 normative semantics are not consistent with OpenSSL's alert mapping:

- RFC 8446 classifies "signatures in the certificate could not be verified correctly" as `bad_certificate`
- OpenSSL maps `X509_V_ERR_CERT_SIGNATURE_FAILURE` to `SSL_AD_DECRYPT_ERROR`
- In the actual reproduction, the client also receives `fatal decrypt_error`

Therefore, the difference here is an inconsistency in alert semantics rather than in the handshake result: the connection is still terminated correctly, and the security boundary is not weakened.

## Normative Basis

RFC 8446 defines `bad_certificate` as follows:

```text
bad_certificate:  A certificate was corrupt, contained signatures
   that did not verify correctly, etc.
```

The phrase `contained signatures that did not verify correctly` directly covers the "certificate signature verification failure" scenario. Therefore, according to the normative text semantics, this kind of error should fall under `bad_certificate`.

## OpenSSL Mapping Basis

In `ssl/statem/statem_lib.c`, OpenSSL defines the mapping from X.509 verification errors to TLS alerts in `x509table[]`. The relevant entries are:

```c
{ X509_V_ERR_CERT_SIGNATURE_FAILURE, SSL_AD_DECRYPT_ERROR },
{ X509_V_ERR_CRL_SIGNATURE_FAILURE,  SSL_AD_DECRYPT_ERROR },
```

That is, `X509_V_ERR_CERT_SIGNATURE_FAILURE` is not mapped to `SSL_AD_BAD_CERTIFICATE`; instead, it is mapped to `SSL_AD_DECRYPT_ERROR`.

In `ssl/statem/statem_srvr.c`, after server-side certificate verification fails, the following code is executed:

```c
SSLfatal(s, ssl_x509err2alert(s->verify_result),
    SSL_R_CERTIFICATE_VERIFY_FAILED);
```

Therefore, as long as `verify_result` is `X509_V_ERR_CERT_SIGNATURE_FAILURE`, the alert ultimately sent is `decrypt_error`.

## Runtime Reproduction Result

The certificate verification log shows:

```text
error 7 at 0 depth lookup: certificate signature failure
```

The handshake log shows:

```text
<<< TLS 1.3, Alert [length 0002], fatal decrypt_error
SSL3 alert read:fatal:decrypt error
... SSL alert number 51
```

This shows that in the same failure:

- The X.509 verification layer has already explicitly identified it as `certificate signature failure`
- The TLS layer ultimately sends the alert `fatal decrypt_error`

## Final Explanation

Based on this, it can be confirmed that there is a verifiable semantic difference here:

- Normative semantics: it should fall under `bad_certificate`
- OpenSSL implementation: it actually sends `decrypt_error`

This kind of difference mainly affects alert semantics, log classification, and standards comparison analysis. It does not affect the result that the handshake fails, nor does it allow certificate verification to be bypassed.

items.body:
# Problem Description:
A segmentation fault occurs when using the `pkey` utility to encrypt a ML-DSA-65 private key if the password is provided interactively. The crash does not occur if `-passout pass:xxx` is used, or if the `pkcs8` utility is used instead.

# Steps to reproduce:
1. Compile openssl from master branch (tested at commit `f023662d1bde1fcb7fecf976b25a45afd55734b8`):
```
sudo ./config --prefix=/usr/local/ssl -d no-asm -O0
sudo make -j$(nproc)
sudo make install
``` 
2. Generate plain key: `/usr/local/ssl/bin/openssl genpkey -algorithm ML-DSA-65 -out plain.pem`
3. Attempt to encrypt it: `/usr/local/ssl/bin/openssl pkey -in plain.pem -aes-256-cbc -out enc.pem`
4. Result of encryption:
```
$ /usr/local/ssl/bin/openssl pkey -in plain.pem -aes-256-cbc -out enc.pem
Segmentation fault
```
## Found workarounds:
1. Use `-passout pass:xxx` instead of providing password interactively:
```
$ ll
total 16K
drwxr-xr-x  2 ca-admin ca-admin 4.0K Apr 18 03:03 .
drwxrwxrwt 11 root     root     4.0K Apr 18 02:47 ..
-rw-------  1 ca-admin ca-admin 5.5K Apr 18 02:47 plain.pem
$ /usr/local/ssl/bin/openssl pkey -in plain.pem -aes-256-cbc -out enc-passout.pem -passout pass:123
$ ll
total 24K
drwxr-xr-x  2 ca-admin ca-admin 4.0K Apr 18 03:08 .
drwxrwxrwt 11 root     root     4.0K Apr 18 02:47 ..
-rw-------  1 ca-admin ca-admin 5.7K Apr 18 03:08 enc-passout.pem
-rw-------  1 ca-admin ca-admin 5.5K Apr 18 02:47 plain.pem
```
2. Use `pkcs8` instead of `pkey`:
```
$ /usr/local/ssl/bin/openssl pkcs8 -topk8 -v2 aes256 -in plain.pem -out enc-pkcs8.pem
Enter Encryption Password:
Verifying - Enter Encryption Password:
$ ll
total 32K
drwxr-xr-x  2 ca-admin ca-admin 4.0K Apr 18 03:08 .
drwxrwxrwt 11 root     root     4.0K Apr 18 02:47 ..

items.title: Segfault in 'openssl pkey' for interactive encryption (v4.0.0)

items.body:
## Summary

`crypto/property/property.c` has a double-free in the property method cache insertion failure path.

In `ossl_method_store_cache_set_locked`, a temporary `QUERY *p` is allocated before insertion into the method cache.
If the first `ossl_ht_cache_QUERY_insert` call fails, the code frees `p` through `impl_cache_free(p)` and then jumps to `err`, where `OPENSSL_free(p)` is called again.

`impl_cache_free` already frees the `QUERY` object, so this path can free the same allocation twice.

## Affected Code

File: `crypto/property/property.c`
Function: `ossl_method_store_cache_set_locked`

Problematic path:

```c
if (!ossl_ht_cache_QUERY_insert(sa->cache, TO_HT_KEY(&key), p, &old)) {
    impl_cache_free(p);
    goto err;
}

...

err:
    res = 0;
    OPENSSL_free(p);

impl_cache_free() ends by freeing the same object:

OPENSSL_free(elem);
```

## Trigger

The double-free path is reached when the first property method cache hash-table insertion fails, for example due to allocation failure while inserting or growing the cache table.

## Impact

This is a memory-safety bug. Expected impact is process crash or heap corruption if the failure path is reached.

Practical exploitability appears constrained because the known trigger requires cache insertion failure, most plausibly under allocation-failure or memory-pressure conditions. Exposure may be higher for applications that allow untrusted inputs to drive many distinct algorithm fetch/property-query/cache operations.

## Severity Estimate

Suggested severity: low to moderate.

Rationale:

- This is a real double-free on an error path.
- The trigger requires hash-table insertion failure.
- A typical TLS peer does not directly control arbitrary OpenSSL property query strings.
- Exploitability depends on allocator behavior, memory pressure, and how the application exposes algorithm fetching.
items.title: Double-free on failed insert in property method cache

items.body:
Hi,

I'm updating Python to OpenSSL 4 and I get a different error between OpenSSL 3 (3.5.4) and OpenSSL 4 (4.0.0) on `SSL_read_ex()` on the TLS client after the TLS server closes the socket.

When the server closes the socket (with `sslconn.shutdown(socket.SHUT_WR); sslconn.close()`), the first `SSL_read_ex()` on the client fails with `SSL_R_UNEXPECTED_EOF_WHILE_READING` (same behavior on OpenSSL 3 and OpenSSL 4):

* `SSL_get_error()=SSL_ERROR_SSL`
* `ERR_GET_LIB()=ERR_LIB_SSL`
* `ERR_GET_REASON()=SSL_R_UNEXPECTED_EOF_WHILE_READING`

This error is helpful. My problem is on the second `SSL_read_ex()` on the client, I get a different error between OpenSSL 3 and OpenSSL 4:

* OpenSSL 3: `SSL_get_error()=SSL_ERROR_SYSCALL`, `ERR_peek_last_error()=0`. Python raises a `SSLEOFError` in this case (behaves correctly).
* OpenSSL 4: `SSL_get_error()=SSL_ERROR_SSL`, `ERR_peek_last_error()=0`. Python raises a generic `SSLError("A failure in the SSL library occurred")` in this case (which is unexpected).

OpenSSL 4 sets a generic `SSL_ERROR_SSL` error with `ERR_peek_last_error()=0`. It's not helpful to detect an "EOF".

My question is if the different error on OpenSSL 4 was made on purpose, or if it's an issue in OpenSSL 4?

---

If you want to reproduce my issue, get the Python script:

<details>

```py
import os.path
import socket
import ssl
import threading

CERT = os.path.join('Lib', 'test', 'certdata', 'keycert.pem')
HOST = '127.0.0.1'
HOSTNAME = 'localhost'

class Server(threading.Thread):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.listening = threading.Event()
        self.address = None

    def run(self):
        context = ssl.create_default_context(ssl.Purpose.CLIENT_AUTH)
        context.load_cert_chain(CERT)
        server_sock = socket.socket(socket.AF_INET, socket.SOCK_STREAM)
        #server_sock.setsockopt(socket.SOL_SOCKET, socket.SO_REUSEADDR, 1)
        #server_sock.setsockopt(socket.SOL_SOCKET, socket.SO_REUSEPORT, 1)
        server_sock.bind((HOST, 0))
        server_sock.listen(5)

        self.address = server_sock.getsockname()
        self.listening.set()

        sock, addr = server_sock.accept()
        sslconn = context.wrap_socket(sock, server_side=True)

        request = b''
        while True:
            chunk = sslconn.recv(65537)
            request += chunk
            if b'\r\n\r\n' in request:
                break
        print(f"server got request: {request!r}")

        print("server sendall")
        sslconn.sendall(
            b'HTTP/1.0 200 OK\r\n'
            b'Server: TestHTTP/ Python/3.15.0a8+\r\n'
            b'Date: Thu, 16 Apr 2026 12:42:37 GMT\r\n'
            b'Content-type: text/plain\r\n\r\n')
        sslconn.sendall(b'we care a bit')

        print("server shutdown write")
        sslconn.shutdown(socket.SHUT_WR)
        print("server close socket")
        sslconn.close()

        server_sock.close()

def main():
    server = Server()
    server.start()
    server.listening.wait()
    port = server.address[1]

    context = ssl.create_default_context(cafile=CERT)
    sock = socket.socket(socket.AF_INET, socket.SOCK_STREAM)
    sock.connect((HOST, port))
    sslsock = context.wrap_socket(sock, server_hostname=HOSTNAME)

    sslsock.sendall(b'GET /bizarre HTTP/1.0\r\n\r\n')
    sslobj = sslsock._sslobj

    def read(prefix, sslobj):
        try:
            data = sslobj.read(1024)
            result = repr(data)
        except ssl.SSLError as exc:
            result = f'<{exc!r}>'
        print(prefix, result)

    for i in range(1, 5):
        read(f"client read #{i}:", sslobj)
    sslsock.close()

    server.join()

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
```

</details>

And run these commands on Linux:

```sh
git clone https://github.com/python/cpython --depth=1
cd cpython/
# Build OpenSSL 4 in ~/multissl/openssl/4.0.0/
python3 Tools/ssl/multissltests.py --steps=library --base-directory $HOME/multissl --openssl 4.0.0 --system Linux
./configure --with-pydebug --with-openssl=$HOME/multissl/openssl/4.0.0/
LD_LIBRARY_PATH=~/multissl/openssl/4.0.0/lib64/ make
LD_LIBRARY_PATH=~/multissl/openssl/4.0.0/lib64/ ./python script.py 
```

Victor

items.body:
OpenSSL 4.0 appears to advertise 27 signature algorithms by default. This appears to be almost every signature algorithm supported minus the SHA-1 based algorithms. If one wishes to remove a single algorithm from this list, they have to specify 26 algorithms.

I think the `SignatureAlgorithms` config option could benefit from supporting a `DEFAULT` keyword similar to other options like `CipherString` and `Groups` do. This would simplify removing algorithms from the list for users as well as make it more feasible to have the default list be something less than the complete set of supported algorithms.

I don't know if you'd want to use `-` like `Groups` does or `!` like `CipherString` does but possible syntax could be something like:
```
# remove an icky algorithm
SignatureAlgorithms = DEFAULT:-icky_sigalg

# or add a niche algorithm to the list
SignatureAlgorithms = DEFAULT:niche_sigalg
```

labels.description: The issue was opened to request a feature
labels.name: issue: feature request

items.title: Support a `DEFAULT` keyword for TLS Signature Algorithms

items.body:
## Summary

`PKCS7_get_issuer_and_serial(PKCS7 *p7, int idx)` does not validate that `idx >= 0`.
The only bounds check is:

```c
/* pk7_doit.c:1174 */
if (sk_PKCS7_RECIP_INFO_num(rsk) <= idx)
    return NULL;
```

For `idx = -1`, the comparison `num <= -1` is **false** (both sides are `int`,
`num >= 0`), so the guard passes. `sk_PKCS7_RECIP_INFO_value(rsk, -1)` returns NULL,
and the next line dereferences it:

```c
ri = sk_PKCS7_RECIP_INFO_value(rsk, idx);   /* returns NULL for idx < 0 */
return ri->issuer_and_serial;               /* NULL dereference → crash */
```


## Environment

- **Version:** OpenSSL 4.0.0 (tag `openssl-4.0.0`, commit `11b7b6e`)
- **OS:** Linux x86_64
- **Compiler:** clang with AddressSanitizer + UndefinedBehaviorSanitizer
- **Build:** `./config no-shared no-tests no-apps --debug CC=clang CFLAGS="-fsanitize=address,undefined -fno-omit-frame-pointer -g -O1"`

## Steps to reproduce

I attached poc_negidx.c

[poc_negidx.c](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/26911308/poc_negidx.c)

```bash
./config no-shared no-tests no-apps --debug \
    CC=clang CFLAGS="-fsanitize=address,undefined -fno-omit-frame-pointer -g -O1"
make -j$(nproc) build_libs

OPENSSL=/tmp/openssl-4.0.0
clang -fsanitize=address,undefined -fno-omit-frame-pointer -g -O1 \
    -I"$OPENSSL/include" poc_negidx.c \
    "$OPENSSL/libssl.a" "$OPENSSL/libcrypto.a" -lpthread -ldl -o poc_negidx
ASAN_OPTIONS="halt_on_error=0:print_stacktrace=1:detect_leaks=0" ./poc_negidx
```

## Expected behavior

`PKCS7_get_issuer_and_serial()` returns `NULL` for any out-of-range index, including
negative values.

## Actual behavior

```
crypto/pkcs7/pk7_doit.c:1177:16: runtime error: member access within null pointer of type 'PKCS7_RECIP_INFO'
==ERROR: AddressSanitizer: SEGV on unknown address 0x000000000008
    #0 PKCS7_get_issuer_and_serial  crypto/pkcs7/pk7_doit.c:1177
    #1 main                         poc_negidx.c
SUMMARY: AddressSanitizer: SEGV pk7_doit.c:1177:16 in PKCS7_get_issuer_and_serial
```

## Suggested fix

Add a lower-bound check before the existing guard:

```diff
 PKCS7_ISSUER_AND_SERIAL *PKCS7_get_issuer_and_serial(PKCS7 *p7, int idx)
 {
     ...
+    if (idx < 0)
+        return NULL;
     if (sk_PKCS7_RECIP_INFO_num(rsk) <= idx)
         return NULL;
     ri = sk_PKCS7_RECIP_INFO_value(rsk, idx);
     return ri->issuer_and_serial;
 }
```

labels.name: issue: bug report

items.body:
## Summary

`EVP_PKEY_CTX_get_rsa_padding()` and `EVP_PKEY_CTX_get_rsa_pss_saltlen()` do not validate that the output-pointer argument is non-NULL before writing to it. Passing `NULL` causes a NULL pointer write (SIGBUS/SEGV) instead of a proper error return.

The affected functions in `crypto/evp/ctrl_params_translate.c`:
- `fix_rsa_padding_mode()` at line 1363: `*(int *)ctx->orig_p2 = str_value_map[i].id;`
- `fix_rsa_pss_saltlen()` at line 1443: `*(int *)ctx->orig_p2 = val;`

`ctx->orig_p2` is set from the caller's `p2` argument during `PRE_CTRL_TO_PARAMS`.
No NULL check precedes either store.

## Environment

- **Version:** OpenSSL 4.0.0 (tag `openssl-4.0.0`, commit `11b7b6e`)
- **OS:** Linux x86_64
- **Compiler:** clang with AddressSanitizer + UndefinedBehaviorSanitizer
- **Build:** `./config no-shared no-tests no-apps --debug CC=clang CFLAGS="-fsanitize=address,undefined -fno-omit-frame-pointer -g -O1"`

## Steps to reproduce

I attached poc_minimal.c

[poc_minimal.c](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/26911588/poc_minimal.c)

```bash
cd /tmp/openssl-4.0.0
./config no-shared no-tests no-apps --debug \
    CC=clang CFLAGS="-fsanitize=address,undefined -fno-omit-frame-pointer -g -O1"
make -j$(nproc) build_libs

OPENSSL=/tmp/openssl-4.0.0
clang -fsanitize=address,undefined -fno-omit-frame-pointer -g -O1 \
    -I"$OPENSSL/include" poc_minimal.c \
    "$OPENSSL/libssl.a" "$OPENSSL/libcrypto.a" -lpthread -ldl -o poc_evp
ASAN_OPTIONS="halt_on_error=0:print_stacktrace=1:detect_leaks=0" ./poc_evp
```

## Expected behavior

`EVP_PKEY_CTX_get_rsa_padding(ctx, NULL)` returns `-1` and sets an error
(`EVP_R_MISSING_PARAMETERS` or similar). Same for `EVP_PKEY_CTX_get_rsa_pss_saltlen`.

## Actual behavior

```
crypto/evp/ctrl_params_translate.c:1363:13: runtime error: store to null pointer of type 'int'
SUMMARY: UndefinedBehaviorSanitizer: undefined-behavior ctrl_params_translate.c:1363:13 in fix_rsa_padding_mode
ERROR: AddressSanitizer: SEGV on unknown address 0x000000000000
SUMMARY: AddressSanitizer: SEGV ctrl_params_translate.c:1363:34 in fix_rsa_padding_mode
```

## Root cause

The ctrl-to-params translation for `EVP_PKEY_CTRL_GET_RSA_PADDING` saves the caller's `p2` into `ctx->orig_p2` without checking for NULL:

```c
/* ctrl_params_translate.c:1280 */
ctx->orig_p2 = ctx->p2;   /* p2 = NULL from caller → orig_p2 = NULL */

/* ... later at POST_CTRL_TO_PARAMS: */
*(int *)ctx->orig_p2 = str_value_map[i].id;  /* write to NULL → crash */
```

The same pattern exists in `fix_rsa_pss_saltlen` at line 1401/1443.

## Suggested fix

Add a NULL guard in both functions, or add it in the public wrapper:

```diff
+/* In EVP_PKEY_CTX_get_rsa_padding() wrapper: */
 int EVP_PKEY_CTX_get_rsa_padding(EVP_PKEY_CTX *ctx, int *pad_mode)
 {
+    if (pad_mode == NULL) {
+        ERR_raise(ERR_LIB_EVP, ERR_R_PASSED_NULL_PARAMETER);
+        return -1;
+    }
     return RSA_pkey_ctx_ctrl(ctx, -1, EVP_PKEY_CTRL_GET_RSA_PADDING, 0, pad_mode);
 }
```

Or alternatively at the `ctrl_params_translate.c` layer:

```diff
 } else if (state == POST_CTRL_TO_PARAMS) {
+    if (ctx->orig_p2 == NULL) {
+        ERR_raise(ERR_LIB_EVP, ERR_R_PASSED_NULL_PARAMETER);
+        return -1;
+    }
     *(int *)ctx->orig_p2 = str_value_map[i].id;
```

labels.description: The issue was opened to report a bug
labels.name: issue: bug report

items.title: NULL pointer write in `EVP_PKEY_CTX_get_rsa_padding()` and `EVP_PKEY_CTX_get_rsa_pss_saltlen()` when output pointer is NULL

items.body:
## Summary

The comment in `crypto/ec/ecp_nistp256.c` (line 2090) explicitly documents the `EC_POINTs_mul` API contract:

> "we treat NULL scalars as 0, and NULL points as points at infinity"

The optimized x86-64 implementation `ecp_nistz256_windowed_mul()` in `crypto/ec/ecp_nistz256.c` does **not** implement this contract. At line 637, `BN_num_bits(scalar[i])` is called unconditionally before any NULL check, causing an immediate crash when `scalar[i] == NULL`.

The inconsistency is between two implementations of the same logical interface:
- `ecp_nistp256.c` (generic): honours the NULL-scalar contract
- `ecp_nistz256.c` (x86-64 optimised): crashes on NULL scalar

## Note on deprecated API

`EC_POINTs_mul()` is marked deprecated since OpenSSL 3.0 and compiled under `#ifndef OPENSSL_NO_DEPRECATED_3_0`. It is still present in OpenSSL 4.0.0. The crash is only reachable through this deprecated function; the active `EC_POINT_mul` path never passes NULL scalars to `ecp_nistz256_points_mul`. Applications using old code that calls `EC_POINTs_mul` with error-path NULL scalars will crash on x86-64.

## Environment

- **Version:** OpenSSL 4.0.0 (tag `openssl-4.0.0`, commit `11b7b6e`)
- **OS:** Linux x86_64
- **Compiler:** clang with AddressSanitizer + UndefinedBehaviorSanitizer
- **Build:** `./config no-shared no-tests no-apps --debug CC=clang CFLAGS="-fsanitize=address,undefined -fno-omit-frame-pointer -g -O1"`

## Steps to reproduce

I attached poc.c

[poc.c](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/26911821/poc.c)

```bash
cd /tmp/openssl-4.0.0
./config no-shared no-tests no-apps --debug \
    CC=clang CFLAGS="-fsanitize=address,undefined -fno-omit-frame-pointer -g -O1"
make -j$(nproc) build_libs

OPENSSL=/tmp/openssl-4.0.0
clang -fsanitize=address,undefined -fno-omit-frame-pointer -g -O1 \
    -I"$OPENSSL/include" poc.c \
    "$OPENSSL/libssl.a" "$OPENSSL/libcrypto.a" -lpthread -ldl -o poc_ec
ASAN_OPTIONS="halt_on_error=0:print_stacktrace=1:detect_leaks=0" ./poc_ec
```

## Expected behavior

`EC_POINTs_mul` with `scalars[i] = NULL` treats the corresponding scalar as zero, consistent with the documented contract and the `ecp_nistp256.c` implementation.

## Actual behavior (on x86-64, which routes through ecp_nistz256)

```
crypto/bn/bn_lib.c:180:16: runtime error: member access within null pointer of type 'const BIGNUM'
SUMMARY: UndefinedBehaviorSanitizer: undefined-behavior bn_lib.c:180:16 in BN_num_bits
ERROR: AddressSanitizer: SEGV on unknown address 0x000000000008
    #0 BN_num_bits                   crypto/bn/bn_lib.c:180
    #1 ecp_nistz256_windowed_mul      crypto/ec/ecp_nistz256.c:637
    #2 ecp_nistz256_points_mul        crypto/ec/ecp_nistz256.c:1124
    #3 EC_POINTs_mul                  crypto/ec/ec_lib.c
    #4 main                           poc.c
SUMMARY: AddressSanitizer: SEGV bn_lib.c:180:16 in BN_num_bits
```

## Vulnerable code

```c
/* ecp_nistz256.c:637 — no NULL check before BN_num_bits */
for (i = 0; i < num; i++) {
    P256_POINT *row = table[i];
    if ((BN_num_bits(scalar[i]) > 256) || BN_is_negative(scalar[i])) {
         /* ^ crashes if scalar[i] == NULL */
```

Compare with `ecp_nistp256.c:2093` which correctly implements the contract:
```c
for (i = 0; i < num_points; ++i) {
    if (scalars[i] == NULL)
        continue;  /* skip — treated as 0 */
```

## Suggested fix

```diff
 for (i = 0; i < num; i++) {
     P256_POINT *row = table[i];
+    if (scalar[i] == NULL)
+        continue;  /* treat NULL scalar as 0 per documented contract */
     if ((BN_num_bits(scalar[i]) > 256) || BN_is_negative(scalar[i])) {
```

Alternatively, `EC_POINTs_mul` could filter NULL scalars before dispatching to the method, providing a single authoritative fix across all backends.

labels.description: The issue was opened to report a bug
labels.name: issue: bug report

items.title: `ecp_nistz256_windowed_mul` crashes on NULL scalar despite documented contract

items.body:
# Undefined Behavior in `SSL_CTX_set_ciphersuites` via Empty TLS 1.3 Ciphersuite Elements

## Summary
An undefined-behavior bug occurs in OpenSSL when `SSL_CTX_set_ciphersuites()` is called with a colon-separated TLS 1.3 ciphersuite string containing an empty element such as `"::"`. During parsing, `CONF_parse_list()` invokes `ciphersuite_cb(NULL, 0, ...)` for the empty list entry, and `ciphersuite_cb()` then passes the null pointer to `memcpy()`, triggering UBSan.

## Version
```
$ git describe
4.0-POST-CLANG-FORMAT-WEBKIT-918-gb8df87aca7
```

## Description
`SSL_CTX_set_ciphersuites()` configures the TLS 1.3 ciphersuite preference list for an `SSL_CTX`. Internally it routes the caller-provided string through the following path:

```text
SSL_CTX_set_ciphersuites()
  -> set_ciphersuites()
    -> CONF_parse_list(str, ':', 1, ciphersuite_cb, ...)
      -> ciphersuite_cb(elem, len, ...)
```

The root cause is a contract mismatch between `CONF_parse_list()` and `ciphersuite_cb()`:

- `CONF_parse_list()` represents an empty list element by calling the callback with `elem = NULL` and `len = 0`.
- `ciphersuite_cb()` assumes `elem` is always non-null and unconditionally executes `memcpy(name, elem, len)`.

Relevant code in `crypto/conf/conf_mod.c`:

```c
if (p == lstart || *lstart == '\0')
    ret = list_cb(NULL, 0, arg);
```

Relevant code in `ssl/ssl_ciph.c`:

```c
static int ciphersuite_cb(const char *elem, int len, void *arg)
{
    STACK_OF(SSL_CIPHER) *ciphersuites = (STACK_OF(SSL_CIPHER) *)arg;
    const SSL_CIPHER *cipher;
    char name[80];

    if (len > (int)(sizeof(name) - 1))
        return 1;

    memcpy(name, elem, len);
    name[len] = '\0';

    cipher = ssl3_get_tls13_cipher_by_std_name(name);
    if (cipher == NULL)
        return 1;

    ...
}
```

This is not just a fuzzer-only misuse pattern. The public API accepts a string and is expected to either configure valid ciphersuites or reject the request cleanly. According to the `SSL_CTX_set_ciphersuites(3)` documentation, unknown or mistyped ciphersuite names are ignored, and the call should fail only if no valid ciphersuite is collected. A malformed string containing an empty element should therefore return failure or skip the empty item, not trigger undefined behavior inside libssl.

In practice, any input containing a double separator is sufficient to reach the bug, for example:

```text
TLS_AES_128_GCM_SHA256::TLS_AES_256_GCM_SHA384
```

When the parser reaches the empty entry between the two colons, it calls `ciphersuite_cb(NULL, 0, ...)`, which immediately reaches the invalid `memcpy()` call.

## PoC Code
```cpp
#include <openssl/ssl.h>
#include <string>

extern "C" int LLVMFuzzerTestOneInput(uint8_t *data, int size) {
    SSL_CTX *ctx = SSL_CTX_new(TLS_method());
    if (ctx != NULL) {
    const char *ciphersuites =
        "TLS_AES_128_GCM_SHA256::TLS_AES_256_GCM_SHA384";
    SSL_CTX_set_ciphersuites(ctx, ciphersuites);
    }
    return 0;
}
```

## Reproduction Step
```bash
clang++ -g -O1 -fno-omit-frame-pointer -fsanitize=address,undefined,fuzzer \
    -I<path-to-openssl>/include \
    poc.cpp -o poc \
    <path-to-openssl>/lib/libssl.a \
    <path-to-openssl>/lib/libcrypto.a \
    -ldl -lpthread

UBSAN_OPTIONS=halt_on_error=1:print_stacktrace=1 ./poc
```

## Stack Trace
```text
ssl/ssl_ciph.c:1241:18: runtime error: null pointer passed as argument 2, which is declared to never be null
/usr/include/string.h:44:28: note: nonnull attribute specified here
    #0 0x5f839960a541 in ciphersuite_cb /root/src/openssl/ssl/ssl_ciph.c:1241:5
    #1 0x5f8399a6d7ef in CONF_parse_list /root/src/openssl/crypto/conf/conf_mod.c:707:19
    #2 0x5f83995f8c2a in set_ciphersuites /root/src/openssl/ssl/ssl_ciph.c:1271:13
    #3 0x5f83995f8990 in SSL_CTX_set_ciphersuites /root/src/openssl/ssl/ssl_ciph.c:1349:15
    #4 0x5f8399578d11 in LLVMFuzzerTestOneInput /poc.cpp:9:5

SUMMARY: UndefinedBehaviorSanitizer: undefined-behavior ssl/ssl_ciph.c:1241:18 in 
```

## Suggested Fix
Add an explicit guard for empty elements before the `memcpy()` call in `ciphersuite_cb()`, for example:

```c
if (elem == NULL || len == 0)
    return 1;
```

Alternatively, if empty list elements should be rejected rather than ignored, the callback can return failure and let `SSL_CTX_set_ciphersuites()` report a parse error. In either case, the library should not reach UB for malformed input.

---
> Signed-off-by: FuzzAnything fuzzanything@gmail.com

labels.description: The issue was opened to report a bug
labels.name: issue: bug report

# Null Pointer Dereference in DH Paramgen Type Translation via `EVP_PKEY_CTX_set_dh_paramgen_type(0)`

## Summary
An UndefinedBehaviorSanitizer-reported null pointer dereference occurs in the DH provider keymgmt path when `EVP_PKEY_CTX_set_dh_paramgen_type()` is called with `type = 0`.

This is not an API misuse issue. `0` is the documented valid constant `DH_PARAMGEN_TYPE_GENERATOR`. The crash is caused by an internal translation bug in the legacy `EVP_PKEY_CTX_ctrl()` to provider-parameter bridge, which constructs an `OSSL_PARAM` UTF8 string with `data == NULL` and then passes it into `strcmp()` inside the DH provider.

## Version
```
$ git describe
4.0-POST-CLANG-FORMAT-WEBKIT-918-gb8df87aca7
```

## Description
The crash occurs when OpenSSL handles the following valid API sequence:

1. Create a DH `EVP_PKEY_CTX`.
2. Initialize it for parameter generation with `EVP_PKEY_paramgen_init()`.
3. Set the DH parameter generation type with `EVP_PKEY_CTX_set_dh_paramgen_type(ctx, 0)`.

The value `0` is explicitly defined in `include/openssl/dh.h` as:

```c
#define DH_PARAMGEN_TYPE_GENERATOR 0 /* Use a safe prime generator */
```

and the manual documents it as a supported value for `EVP_PKEY_CTX_set_dh_paramgen_type()`.

The root cause is an internal mismatch between the legacy ctrl-style API and the provider-side parameter format:

- `EVP_PKEY_CTX_set_dh_paramgen_type()` forwards the integer `typ` through `EVP_PKEY_CTX_ctrl()`.
- The ctrl-to-provider translation table maps this control to `OSSL_PKEY_PARAM_FFC_TYPE`, whose provider-side representation is `OSSL_PARAM_UTF8_STRING`.
- The fixup helper `fix_dh_paramgen_type()` only converts values for the `PRE_CTRL_STR_TO_PARAMS` path, where input arrives as a string.
- For the normal `PRE_CTRL_TO_PARAMS` path used by `EVP_PKEY_CTX_set_dh_paramgen_type(ctx, 0)`, no integer-to-string conversion is performed.
- As a result, the generic translator constructs a UTF8 `OSSL_PARAM` using `ctx->p2 == NULL`.
- The DH provider receives a non-NULL `OSSL_PARAM *type`, but `type->data` is NULL, and `dh_gen_type_name2id_w_default()` calls `strcmp(name, "default")` without checking for NULL first.

This produces the sanitizer finding:

```c
static int dh_gen_type_name2id_w_default(const char *name, int type)
{
    if (strcmp(name, "default") == 0) {
        ...
    }
    return ossl_dh_gen_type_name2id(name, type);
}
```

and the provider-side caller also lacks a NULL check on `p->type->data`:

```c
if (p->type != NULL) {
    if (p->type->data_type != OSSL_PARAM_UTF8_STRING
        || ((gen_type = dh_gen_type_name2id_w_default(p->type->data,
                                                      gctx->dh_type)) == -1)) {
        ERR_raise(ERR_LIB_PROV, ERR_R_PASSED_INVALID_ARGUMENT);
        return 0;
    }
}
```

In other words, a valid public API value is transformed into an invalid internal provider parameter, and the provider then dereferences it unsafely. 

## PoC Code
```cpp
#include <openssl/dh.h>
#include <openssl/evp.h>

extern "C" int LLVMFuzzerTestOneInput(uint8_t *data, int size) {
  EVP_PKEY_CTX *ctx = EVP_PKEY_CTX_new_id(EVP_PKEY_DH, NULL);
  (void)EVP_PKEY_paramgen_init(ctx);
  EVP_PKEY_CTX_set_dh_paramgen_type(ctx, 0);
}
```

## Reproduction Step
Build a small harness against the affected OpenSSL tree with UBSan enabled:

```bash
clang++ -g -O1 -fno-omit-frame-pointer -fsanitize=address,undefined,fuzzer \
    -I/openssl/include \
    poc.cpp -o poc \
    /openssl/libcrypto.a \
    -ldl -lpthread
```

Then run:

```bash
export UBSAN_OPTIONS=halt_on_error=1:print_stacktrace=1
./poc
```

## Stack Trace
```text
providers/implementations/keymgmt/dh_kmgmt.c:89:16: runtime error: null pointer passed as argument 1, which is declared to never be null
/usr/include/string.h:157:33: note: nonnull attribute specified here
    #0 0x64863b8e58b8 in dh_gen_type_name2id_w_default /root/src/openssl/providers/implementations/keymgmt/dh_kmgmt.c:89:9
    #1 0x64863b8e4dc4 in dh_gen_common_set_params /root/src/openssl/providers/implementations/keymgmt/dh_kmgmt.c:626:29
    #2 0x64863b8dd523 in dh_gen_set_params /root/src/openssl/providers/implementations/keymgmt/dh_kmgmt.c:724:10
    #3 0x64863bdb084c in evp_keymgmt_gen_set_params /root/src/openssl/crypto/evp/keymgmt_meth.c:424:12
    #4 0x64863b59d6fd in EVP_PKEY_CTX_set_params /root/src/openssl/crypto/evp/pmeth_lib.c:446:20
    #5 0x64863b5a4c4b in evp_pkey_ctx_set_params_strict /root/src/openssl/crypto/evp/pmeth_lib.c:629:12
    #6 0x64863bd30c49 in evp_pkey_ctx_ctrl_to_param /root/src/openssl/crypto/evp/ctrl_params_translate.c:2774:19
    #7 0x64863b5a9c51 in evp_pkey_ctx_ctrl_int /root/src/openssl/crypto/evp/pmeth_lib.c:1063:16
    #8 0x64863b5a5a98 in EVP_PKEY_CTX_ctrl /root/src/openssl/crypto/evp/pmeth_lib.c:1097:12
    #9 0x64863b58b726 in EVP_PKEY_CTX_set_dh_paramgen_type /root/src/openssl/crypto/evp/dh_ctrl.c:86:12
    #10 0x64863b58ac9a in LLVMFuzzerTestOneInput /poc.cpp:7:3


SUMMARY: UndefinedBehaviorSanitizer: undefined-behavior providers/implementations/keymgmt/dh_kmgmt.c:89:16 in 
```

## Suggested Fix
The primary fix should be in the ctrl-to-provider translation layer:

- Update `fix_dh_paramgen_type()` in `crypto/evp/ctrl_params_translate.c` to handle the `PRE_CTRL_TO_PARAMS` path.
- Convert the integer enum in `ctx->p1` to the corresponding provider string using `ossl_dh_gen_type_id2name()` before constructing the `OSSL_PARAM`.

Additionally, the provider should be hardened:

- In `providers/implementations/keymgmt/dh_kmgmt.c`, reject `p->type->data == NULL` before calling `dh_gen_type_name2id_w_default()`.

That combination would both fix the root cause and prevent similar internal translation bugs from surfacing as undefined behavior.

---
> Signed-off-by: FuzzAnything fuzzanything@gmail.com

labels.description: The issue was opened to report a bug
labels.name: issue: bug report

items.title: Null Pointer Dereference in DH Paramgen Type Translation via `EVP_PKEY_CTX_set_dh_paramgen_type(0)`

items.body:
# Undefined Behavior in `X509_verify` on Incomplete `X509` Objects via `OBJ_cmp`

## Summary
`X509_verify()` can trigger undefined behavior when it is called on an allocated but incomplete `X509` object created by `X509_new()`. The function unconditionally compares two embedded `X509_ALGOR` structures and eventually passes `NULL` `ASN1_OBJECT->data` pointers to `memcmp()` through `OBJ_cmp()`, violating the documented error-handling contract for incomplete certificates.

## Version
```
$ git describe
4.0-POST-CLANG-FORMAT-WEBKIT-918-gb8df87aca7
```

## Description
OpenSSL contains a robustness bug in the certificate signature verification path:

`X509_verify()` in `crypto/x509/x_all.c` first checks that the outer certificate signature algorithm (`x->sig_alg`) matches the TBSCertificate signature algorithm (`x->cert_info.signature`). It performs this check by calling `X509_ALGOR_cmp()`, which immediately calls `OBJ_cmp(a->algorithm, b->algorithm)`.

For a freshly allocated certificate returned by `X509_new()`, these embedded algorithm identifiers may still be in an uninitialized semantic state for verification purposes. In the observed crash, both algorithm objects resolve to an object with `length == 0` and `data == NULL`. `OBJ_cmp()` then executes:

```c
return memcmp(a->data, b->data, a->length);
```

Even though the compared length is zero, passing `NULL` as the pointer argument to `memcmp()` is still undefined behavior under UBSan's nonnull checking.

The vulnerable code path is:

`LLVMFuzzerTestOneInput()` -> `X509_verify()` -> `X509_ALGOR_cmp()` -> `OBJ_cmp()` -> `memcmp()`

The relevant source locations are:

- `crypto/x509/x_all.c:37`
- `crypto/asn1/x_algor.c:101`
- `crypto/objects/obj_lib.c:62`

This is not just a meaningless-fuzzer-input issue. The `X509_verify(3)` documentation explicitly states that if the certificate is ill-formed or not complete, the function should return `-1`. Instead, the current implementation reaches undefined behavior before reporting an error. In other words, the PoC uses an invalid verification scenario, but the library still fails to reject it safely.

## PoC Code
```cpp
#include <openssl/x509.h>

extern "C" int LLVMFuzzerTestOneInput(uint8_t *data, int size) {
  EVP_PKEY *pkey = EVP_PKEY_new();
  X509 *cert = X509_new();
  X509_verify(cert, pkey);
}
```

## Reproduction Step

```
clang++ -g -O1 -fno-omit-frame-pointer -fsanitize=address,undefined,fuzzer \
    -I<path-to-openssl>/include \
    poc.cpp -o poc \
    <path-to-openssl.a>

export UBSAN_OPTIONS=halt_on_error=1:print_stacktrace=1
./poc
```

## Stack Trace
```text
crypto/objects/obj_lib.c:62:19: runtime error: null pointer passed as argument 1, which is declared to never be null
/usr/include/string.h:65:33: note: nonnull attribute specified here
    #0 0x64c6a3e05f82 in OBJ_cmp /root/src/openssl/crypto/objects/obj_lib.c:62:12
    #1 0x64c6a4308f89 in X509_ALGOR_cmp /root/src/openssl/crypto/asn1/x_algor.c:101:10
    #2 0x64c6a3e5b81d in X509_verify /root/src/openssl/crypto/x509/x_all.c:37:9
    #3 0x64c6a3d60c9b in LLVMFuzzerTestOneInput /poc.cpp:6:3
SUMMARY: UndefinedBehaviorSanitizer: undefined-behavior crypto/objects/obj_lib.c:62:19 in 


```

## Suggested Fix Direction
Any of the following would address the bug safely:

- Add explicit validation in `X509_verify()` before calling `X509_ALGOR_cmp()`.
- Make `X509_ALGOR_cmp()` reject `NULL` `algorithm` members safely.
- Make `OBJ_cmp()` return equality for zero-length objects before calling `memcmp()`, or otherwise guard `NULL` data pointers when length is zero.


---
> Signed-off-by: FuzzAnything fuzzanything@gmail.com
items.title: Undefined Behavior in `X509_verify` on Incomplete `X509` Objects via `OBJ_cmp`

items.body:
## Summary

`openssl s_client -quiet` silently drops the TLS session and discards the already-read payload when a single stdin read returns exactly `BUFSIZZ` (16384) bytes.

The length check in `user_data_add()` was tightened from `>` to `>=` in 3.6.2 to reserve a NUL terminator byte:

```c
/* apps/s_client.c:3930 — 3.6.2 */
if (user_data->buflen != 0 || i >= user_data->bufmax)
    return 0;
```

The reserved NUL is only written in `USER_DATA_MODE_ADVANCED` (`cmd_start[outlen] = '\0'` at `apps/s_client.c:4054`), but the guard runs in all modes. In `USER_DATA_MODE_BASIC` (the default) and `USER_DATA_MODE_NONE` (used with `-quiet`), no NUL is ever written, so a legitimate `BUFSIZZ`-sized read from `raw_read_stdin()` is rejected; the caller at `apps/s_client.c:3334` then does `goto shut;` and tears down the session with zero bytes delivered:

```c
/* apps/s_client.c:3334 */
if (i > 0 && !user_data_add(&user_data, i)) {
    ret = 0;
    goto shut;
}
```

Introduced in 3.6.2 by [`e0e276b5`](https://github.com/openssl/openssl/commit/e0e276b50a1eb410a15100d6fe889fceaab16793) ("Fix a one byte buffer overflow in s_client"). Reproduces on `master` and `openssl-3.{3,4,5,6}`; does not reproduce on 3.2 or earlier. Not a security issue (per the referenced commit message).

## Environment

- **Version:** OpenSSL 3.6.2 (tag `openssl-3.6.2`)
- **OS:** macOS 26.4.1 (Darwin 25.4.0), Apple M5, arm64
- **Compiler:** Apple clang (Xcode toolchain)
- **Build:** Homebrew `openssl@3` bottle, `platform: darwin64-arm64-cc`, `options: bn(64,64)`. Also reproduces against a stock `./Configure` build of the `openssl-3.6.2` tag.

## Steps to reproduce

Save as `repro.sh` and run with `OPENSSL=/path/to/openssl ./repro.sh`:

```sh
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -uo pipefail
OPENSSL=${OPENSSL:-openssl}
TMP=$(mktemp -d); SERVER_PID=""
trap '[ -n "$SERVER_PID" ] && kill -9 "$SERVER_PID" 2>/dev/null; rm -rf "$TMP"' EXIT

"$OPENSSL" req -x509 -newkey rsa:2048 -keyout "$TMP/k.pem" -out "$TMP/c.pem" \
  -days 1 -nodes -subj "/CN=localhost" 2>/dev/null
PORT=44433
while lsof -iTCP:$PORT -sTCP:LISTEN -t >/dev/null 2>&1; do PORT=$((PORT+1)); done

test_size() {
  local size=$1; local recv="$TMP/recv.bin"; : > "$recv"
  ( sleep 10 ) | "$OPENSSL" s_server -4 -accept $PORT -cert "$TMP/c.pem" \
    -key "$TMP/k.pem" -quiet -naccept 1 > "$recv" 2>/dev/null &
  SERVER_PID=$!
  while ! lsof -iTCP:$PORT -sTCP:LISTEN -t >/dev/null 2>&1; do sleep 0.1; done
  ( head -c "$size" /dev/zero | tr '\0' A; sleep 1 ) | \
    "$OPENSSL" s_client -4 -connect 127.0.0.1:$PORT -quiet >/dev/null 2>&1
  sleep 0.5; kill -9 $SERVER_PID 2>/dev/null; wait $SERVER_PID 2>/dev/null; SERVER_PID=""
  local got=$(wc -c < "$recv" | tr -d ' ')
  printf "  stdin=%-6d server_received=%-6s %s\n" "$size" "$got" \
    "$([ "$got" = "$size" ] && echo OK || echo FAIL)"
}
for s in 8192 16383 16384 16385 20000; do test_size "$s"; done
```

## Expected behavior

All sizes pass through; `server_received` equals `stdin` for every row (as observed on 3.6.1).

## Actual behavior

```
  stdin=8192   server_received=8192   OK
  stdin=16383  server_received=16383  OK
  stdin=16384  server_received=0      FAIL
  stdin=16385  server_received=0      FAIL
  stdin=20000  server_received=0      FAIL
```

`s_client` exits 0 with no error on stdout or stderr.

## Suggested fix

Restore the `>` check in `user_data_add()` and move the NUL reservation into the ADVANCED-mode branch of `user_data_process()` where the NUL is actually written:

```diff
 static int user_data_add(struct user_data_st *user_data, size_t i)
 {
-    /*
-     * We must allow one byte for a NUL terminator so i must be less than
-     * bufmax
-     */
-    if (user_data->buflen != 0 || i >= user_data->bufmax)
+    if (user_data->buflen != 0 || i > user_data->bufmax)
         return 0;
```

labels.description: The issue/pr is a regression from previous released version
labels.name: severity: regression

items.body:
Many pearl scripts in this repo contain the following text, stating that either the OpenSSL license OR the Cryptograms license apply "**_depending on where you obtain it_**": 
 

> # Written by Andy Polyakov <appro@openssl.org> for the OpenSSL
> # project. The module is, however, dual licensed under OpenSSL and
> # CRYPTOGAMS licenses depending on where you obtain it. For further
> # details see http://www.openssl.org/~appro/cryptogams/.

Aside the broken link discussion in previous threads which seems to have been fixed, could it be explained what this actually means? 

Does it mean that if I were to pull the code from https://github.com/dot-asm/cryptogams, the cryptograms license applies, and if I pull it from this repo here (or one of its forks), the OpenSSL license applies? The latter although this repo might have pulled the code as well from the cryptograms repo, which I don't know? 
For compliance reasons it would be great to have clarity which license applies to those files. Any hint is welcome. 

In addition, for instance in https://github.com/openssl/openssl/blob/master/crypto/chacha/asm/chacha-armv8.pl the reference in the file header has been changed from OpenSLL license to Apache. This makes it even more unclear, given there are now three potentially applicable licenses (although only the copyright holder to the pearl script has the authority to decide under which license the code can be used). 

Apologies if there is a Q/A already, I could not find it.
items.title: Application of CRYPTOGRAMS license unclear

items.body:
Description:
I am reporting a build failure encountered when compiling the OpenSSL 4.0.0 final release on a host using GCC 7 (specifically x86_64-suse-linux/7).

Error Message:
The build fails during the compilation of AVX2-optimized code:
crypto/evp/enc_b64_avx2.c: error: implicit declaration of function ‘_mm256_set_m128i’

Technical Context:
The file crypto/evp/enc_b64_avx2.c utilizes the _mm256_set_m128i() intrinsic. This intrinsic was introduced in GCC 8, making it unavailable for older toolchains like GCC 7. While the [OpenSSL 4.0.0 release notes](https://openssl-library.org/post/2026-04-14-openssl-40-final-release/) mention a move toward [C99 compliance](https://sourcegraph.com/github.com/openssl/openssl/-/blob/NEWS.md), there is no explicit mention that the minimum GCC version has effectively been raised to GCC 8 for x86_64 builds using default optimizations.
Request for Clarification/Action:

Documentation: Should the release notes or INSTALL.md be updated to reflect this higher compiler requirement for x86_64 AVX2 support?

Build System: Is there a planned fix for the Configure script to detect the absence of this intrinsic and gracefully fall back to non-AVX2 code for GCC 7 users?

Environment Details:
OpenSSL Version: 4.0.0 Final
OS: SUSE Linux
Compiler: GCC 7.x
Architecture: x86_64
items.title: Build Failure on GCC 7: Missing Intrinsic _mm256_set_m128i in OpenSSL 4.0.0
